+ Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 81.6
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The hinges of the underhold door gave a tired, iron sigh as Aya pushed it inward. The sound seemed far too loud. Beyond lay a long brick-vaulted chamber sunk beneath the guildhall like the throat of some buried leviathan. Moisture beaded on the ceiling arches. Whale-oil lamps hung at intervals, their glass chimneys smoked half black, their light thick and jaundiced. Brass pipes ran along the walls, hissing faintly as they carried heat from the boilers above. The air smelled of salt, tar, machine grease—and beneath it, older things: dust, old wood, and the dry, bitter scent of preserved bone. Aya stepped first across the threshold. Katra followed, light on her feet despite the pistol at her hip and the pair of knives tucked into her belt. Simeon came last, stooping slightly beneath the arch, his coat heavy with tools and quiet little contraptions that clinked softly when he moved. Too quiet. Aya felt it at once. The stillness had shape. Weight. Her hand hovered near the inside of her coat where the small obsidian token rested cold against her ribs. Katra muttered under her breath. “Don’t like this.” Simeon gave a thin smile. “You never like anything subterranean.” “Subterranean’s where people put traps.” Aya barely heard them. Her eyes had settled on the center of the chamber. The artifact vault stood there as expected—a cylindrical brass reliquary set upon a stone dais, its surface covered in ancient scythan script that twisted like barnacles over a shipwreck. Three locking rings encircled its middle. Runes glowed faintly along the floor around it, warding glyphs scratched deep into the stone. But they were not alone. A chair had been placed before the dais. And in it sat the woman who was supposed to be rotting behind iron bars. Guildmaster Eliza Morska regarded them with mild patience, one gloved hand resting upon the head of a black cane. The silver pommel was shaped like a whale’s skull. She was not alone. Six men stood along the chamber walls. Dock-hardened shoulders beneath long coats, brass pistols visible, blades at their hips. Their faces held the flat readiness of men who had been waiting some time. Aya stopped. Behind her, Simeon whispered softly. “Well. That’s unfortunate.” Katra’s hand slid slowly toward her pistol. Morska tilted her head slightly. “I would advise against that.” The words were gentle. Six pistols lifted with quiet mechanical clicks. Katra froze. For a moment no one spoke. Only the soft hiss of the pipes filled the chamber. Then Morska sighed. “You know,” she said, “I had expected ambition. That is the marrow of this profession.” Her eyes moved across them lazily. “But I confess I did not expect it to be quite so… punctual.” Aya felt the weight of those pale eyes settle on her mask. “Midnight conspirators creeping into my underhold,” Morska continued. “Breaking seals. Seeking relics older than the Empire itself.” She smiled faintly. “It would almost be flattering if it were not so tedious.” Katra spat on the floor. “You’re supposed to be in irons.” “Mm.” Morska tapped the cane once on the stone. “Yes. That was the rumor, wasn’t it?” Simeon rubbed the bridge of his nose. “Let me guess,” he said. “Constables on your payroll.” Morska looked delighted. “Oh, my dear engineer. The constables are everyone’s payroll.” Two of the guards stepped forward. Aya did not resist when her arms were seized. Neither did Katra, though she muttered several creative obscenities while her pistol was taken. Simeon surrendered with theatrical politeness. Within moments they were disarmed and forced to their knees before the dais. Aya’s pulse beat calmly in her ears. Do not reach for the immateria. Not yet. The chamber’s wards crawled along her skin like cold insects. She could feel them suppressing the familiar whispering presence just beyond the veil. Morska rose slowly from the chair. Her boots clicked softly across the stone until she stood before Aya. “Well then,” she said. “Let us see the face of the evening’s architect.” Aya held still. The guildmaster leaned forward and lifted the mask away. Cold air touched Aya’s cheeks. There was a brief silence. One of the guards gave a quiet whistle. Morska’s eyebrows rose. “My word.” Aya watched the recognition bloom. “Lady Aya Virelle,” Morska said softly. “Daughter of the late Magistrate Virelle.” Aya said nothing. Morska circled her slowly, examining her as if she were some curious specimen pinned beneath glass. “Aristocracy slumming in my guild,” she murmured. “How deliciously scandalous.” Katra looked over sharply. “You serious?” “Oh yes,” Morska said. “Quite serious.” Aya exhaled slowly. This is how it ends. Her father’s name dragged through the mud. The estate seized. Her sister ruined. And herself— A rope. Just like mother. The thought passed through her mind with strange calm. Morska crouched before her. “I must admit,” she said, “I had wondered which of my promising young thieves possessed such… unusual talents.” Aya’s stomach tightened. “You speak of things you do not understand,” Aya said quietly. “On the contrary,” said Morska. Her smile thinned. “I understand perfectly why the Crown hanged your mother.” The words were soft as frost. Aya felt something cold stir behind her ribs. Do not. Not here. Not yet. Morska straightened. “So tell me, Lady Virelle. Were you planning to steal the scythan relic and crown yourself queen of thieves?” Aya lifted her chin. “No.” “Mm?” “I was planning,” Aya said, “to save you the trouble of losing it to someone less competent.” One of the guards chuckled. Morska studied her. Then she laughed—a low, genuine sound. “Oh, you are magnificent.” She turned slightly. “What shall we do with them, gentlemen?” A guard answered immediately. “Drown ’em in the harbor.” Another said, “Boiler room would be quicker.” Aya spoke before the decision settled. “Kill us,” she said calmly, “and you lose the most valuable hostage in this city.” Morska paused. Aya forced her voice steady. “You know who my father is. What he sits on. Trade permits. Naval contracts. Half the harbor tariffs.” The guildmaster’s eyes narrowed with interest. Aya continued. “And if word reached him that his beloved daughter had been taken by the Morska guild…” She allowed a thin breath of a smile. “Imagine what he might pay to keep that quiet.” Katra turned slightly toward her. “Hostage?” she whispered. Aya ignored her. Inside, her thoughts churned. If Father learns what I am— Her throat tightened. He will finish what the magistrates began. Morska tapped the cane against the stone again, thinking. “You assume,” she said slowly, “that I care about your father’s reputation.” Aya met her gaze. “No,” she said. “I assume you care about leverage.” Silence stretched. The pipes hissed. Then— Across the harbor, far beyond the guild walls, a foghorn sounded. Once. The mournful call rolled through the stone like the voice of some titanic beast beneath black water. Twice. The guards shifted uneasily. Three times. The sound lingered in the chamber’s damp air. Morska watched Aya for a long moment. Then she smiled again. “Oh, Lady Virelle,” she said. “You may yet prove worth more alive than dead.” :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric worldbuilding within the whalepunk fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a vivid Victorian-gothic atmosphere through detailed descriptions of the underhold chamber, with its whale-oil lamps, brass pipes, and moisture-beaded arches that evoke the belly of a leviathan. The industrial elements blend seamlessly with the magical undertones. The dialogue feels authentic to the setting and characters, with Morska's measured, predatory speech contrasting effectively with Katra's blunt pragmatism and Simeon's dry wit. Aya's voice carries appropriate aristocratic bearing even under duress. The revelation of her identity creates genuine tension and raises the stakes meaningfully. Character actions remain believable throughout. Aya's calculated attempt to leverage her aristocratic status as a bargaining chip feels true to both her background and desperate circumstances. Morska's patient, almost theatrical approach to the confrontation establishes her as a formidable antagonist who enjoys psychological manipulation. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from the ominous quiet of their entry to the shock of discovery to the negotiation for survival. The author handles the "no fight scene" constraint well, focusing instead on psychological warfare and verbal sparring. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the "supposed to be imprisoned but actually waiting in ambush" setup, while executed competently, follows familiar patterns. The ending, while appropriately dark, doesn't quite achieve the full gothic horror atmosphere promised in the prompt. The prose occasionally veers toward purple territory with phrases like "the throat of some buried leviathan" and "the voice of some titanic beast beneath black water," though these generally serve the atmospheric goals. The internal monologue integration works well, particularly Aya's mounting dread about her father's potential reaction. The piece successfully incorporates all required story beats while maintaining narrative coherence and character consistency. The worldbuilding details about constables on payrolls and the tension between technology and magic feel organic to the setting. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 7 Overwrought: 6 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 18.0, Believable Character Actions: 17.0, Nuanced Characters: 16.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 15.0, Emotionally Engaging: 16.0, Emotionally Complex: 15.0, Coherent: 18.0, Meandering: 3.0, Weak Dialogue: 2.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 7.0, Overwrought: 6.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 16.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 17.0, Overall Impression: 16.0
+ Fanfic-crossover: Gunpowder Rendezvous — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Gunpowder Rendezvous Spike Spiegel & Revy Write a story where Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop) and Revy (Black Lagoon) meet in a seedy bar after both being hired for the same bounty. Their mutual attraction is complicated by their trust issues and competitive instincts. Include drinking, gunplay, and unresolved sexual tension that culminates in a motel room standoff. **Spike Spiegel (Cowboy Bebop)** Spike is a laid-back bounty hunter with a dark past as a syndicate enforcer. He practices Jeet Kune Do and is exceptionally skilled with firearms. He's chronically broke, constantly hungry, and addicted to cigarettes. Despite his carefree attitude, he's haunted by his past and the woman he left behind. He has a prosthetic eye from a past injury that affects his depth perception. He's fatalistic to the extreme, often saying he's already died once. He values freedom above all else and dislikes attachments, though he secretly cares deeply for his crew. Famous quote: "Whatever happens, happens." **Revy/Rebecca Lee (Black Lagoon)** Revy is a Chinese-American dual-wielding gunslinger with unparalleled shooting skills. She's foul-mouthed, chain-smoking, and has severe anger management issues stemming from childhood abuse. She's known as "Two Hands" for her ability to accurately fire two pistols simultaneously. She drinks heavily and solves most problems with violence. Despite her hardened exterior, she has moments of vulnerability, particularly around Rock. She has a dragon tattoo covering her right arm and typically wears extremely short shorts and a black crop top regardless of the situation. Famous quote: "I'm not a good person, not a bad person. I'm just here." Third person, present tense. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The bar smells like spilled beer, gun oil, and cheap disinfectant that never quite does its job. A neon jukebox in the corner keeps playing the same scratchy rock track on repeat. The song’s halfway decent the first time. By the fifth, it’s a threat. Spike Spiegel sits slouched at the bar like gravity is optional. One long leg hooked over the stool rung, cigarette dangling from his lips, eyes half-lidded. A glass of whiskey sweats beside his elbow. He looks like a man killing time. Which he is. Across the room, a door bangs open. Revy walks in like she owns every miserable square inch of the place. Black crop top. Short shorts. Boots that look like they’ve kicked in a lot of doors. The dragon tattoo coils down her arm like it’s ready to bite someone. Two pistols ride low at her hips. Her eyes sweep the room. They stop on Spike. Spike exhales smoke slowly. “Hey,” he says lazily, without turning. “You gonna keep staring or buy a drink?” Revy walks over, boots heavy on the floorboards. She drops onto the stool next to him and signals the bartender with two fingers. “Whiskey,” she says. Her eyes flick sideways. “You Spike Spiegel?” Spike tilts his head. “Depends. Who’s asking?” She grins. It’s not a friendly grin. “Name’s Revy.” Spike pauses. “Oh,” he says. “That’s a problem.” The bartender sets down her glass. She knocks half of it back in one swallow. “Yeah,” she says, wiping her mouth with the back of her hand. “It is.” The jukebox restarts the same damn song again. Guitar squeal. Scratchy drums. Spike sighs. “So,” he says. “Let me guess. Bald guy. Burn scar across his face. Fifty million woolongs.” Revy lights a cigarette. “You’re missing the part where I collect it.” Spike finally turns toward her. Up close, her eyes are sharp. Dangerous. A little wild. “Funny,” he says. “I was thinking the same thing.” Silence. They drink. The bar hums around them—low conversations, glasses clinking, the jukebox wheezing through its cursed playlist again. Revy taps ash into the tray. “You planning to shoot me?” she asks. Spike shrugs. “Hadn’t decided yet.” “You should decide quick.” “Oh?” She smiles again. “Because I already decided about you.” Spike chuckles softly. “Yeah?” he says. “And?” Revy drains her glass. “You’re in my way.” Spike studies her. Not nervous. Not bluffing. Just stating a fact. “Tell you what,” he says. “We split it.” Revy barks a laugh. “Split it? Fifty-fifty?” “Sure.” “Are you stupid or just broke?” Spike considers. “Little of both.” The jukebox restarts again. Revy groans. “Jesus Christ.” Spike glances over. “That thing stuck?” Bartender shrugs. “Coin slot’s busted.” Revy rubs her temples. “If I hear that guitar riff one more—” The front window explodes. Gunfire tears through the bar. Spike and Revy move at the same time. Chairs flip. Glass shatters. Spike kicks the barstool aside and slides behind an overturned table. Revy vaults the counter, already pulling both pistols. Outside, three men with submachine guns spray the room. “Your friends?” Spike calls. “Not my style!” Revy fires twice. Two clean shots. One gunman drops instantly. Spike rolls, draws his Jericho, fires through the window frame. Another man goes down. The third dives behind a car. Silence. Smoke drifts through the broken glass. The jukebox keeps playing. Revy stands slowly, guns still raised. “Well,” she mutters. Spike stands too, dusting glass off his suit. “That settles it.” Revy eyes him. “Settles what?” “We’re definitely after the same guy.” Sirens wail faintly in the distance. Revy holsters one pistol. “Bounty’s holed up at the Starlight Motel,” she says. Spike raises an eyebrow. “You talk a lot for someone who wanted to shoot me five minutes ago.” “Don’t flatter yourself.” She grabs another bottle from behind the bar and takes it with her. “C’mon, cowboy.” — The Starlight Motel looks like it rents rooms by the hour and regrets it immediately. A flickering VACANCY sign buzzes overhead. Room 12. The door hangs half open. Inside, the bounty lies unconscious on the floor, zip-tied. Revy kicks the door shut behind them. “Mine,” she says. Spike leans against the dresser. “Pretty sure I helped.” “Pretty sure you didn’t do enough.” They stare at each other. The room smells like stale smoke and cheap detergent. Revy sets the bottle on the table and pours two glasses. Spike takes one. “Temporary truce?” he asks. “Temporary.” They drink. The tension in the room shifts. Not relaxed. Just… different. Spike watches her over the rim of his glass. Revy watches him right back. “Gotta say,” Spike murmurs. “You shoot pretty well.” Revy smirks. “Damn right.” Beat. “You move pretty well too.” Spike shrugs. “Used to be a living.” The neon from outside bleeds through the blinds, striping the room red. Revy steps closer. “Here’s the problem,” she says. “Oh?” “You’re still competition.” Spike nods slowly. “Yeah.” Revy’s hand moves. One pistol clears the holster. Spike’s gun appears in his hand just as fast. Now they’re standing three feet apart. Guns raised. Neither blinking. The unconscious bounty snores softly on the carpet. The jukebox song from the bar echoes faintly from down the street, drifting through the motel walls like a ghost. Revy tilts her head. “You always this calm with a gun pointed at you?” Spike smiles lazily. “Usually there’s dinner first.” Revy laughs. Low. Dangerous. “You’re insane.” “Been called worse.” Neither lowers their weapon. Seconds stretch. Revy’s eyes flick briefly to Spike’s. Not hostile. Not exactly friendly either. Just… charged. “Tell you what,” Spike says quietly. “Yeah?” “We both walk out.” “And the bounty?” “Coin flip.” Revy considers. Then snorts. “You’re unbelievable.” Spike shrugs. “Whatever happens—” “—happens,” she finishes. Silence again. The tension in the room feels like a live wire. Revy slowly lowers her gun. Spike lowers his a second later. They both keep watching each other. Revy grabs the whiskey bottle and takes a long drink straight from it. Then she tosses it to him. Spike catches it. “Same job again sometime?” he asks. Revy heads for the door. “Maybe.” She opens it, pauses, glances back. “Next time,” she says, “I’m not splitting the bounty.” Spike takes a swig. “Next time,” he says, “I won’t let you win.” Revy grins. Then she’s gone. Spike looks down at the unconscious bounty. Outside, somewhere down the street, the same scratchy rock song starts playing again. Spike sighs. “Man,” he mutters. “Not that song again.” :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully captures the essence of both characters while delivering a tightly constructed narrative that hits all the requested elements. The author demonstrates a strong understanding of Spike's laconic fatalism and Revy's volatile aggression, translating their distinctive personalities into authentic dialogue and actions. The prose style is notably economical and effective, using short, punchy sentences that mirror the noir atmosphere of both source materials. The repetitive jukebox motif serves as both atmospheric detail and structural device, creating continuity between scenes while emphasizing the seedy bar setting. The writing maintains consistent third-person present tense throughout. Character voices are well-differentiated and authentic. Spike's casual philosophical detachment comes through in lines like "Whatever happens, happens" and his general unflappability under pressure. Revy's directness and underlying volatility are captured in her blunt dialogue and quick resort to violence. The sexual tension builds naturally through their verbal sparring and mutual recognition of each other's skills. The action sequences are handled with appropriate brevity and clarity. The bar shootout serves multiple narrative functions - establishing external threat, showcasing both characters' combat abilities, and forcing temporary cooperation. The pacing accelerates effectively from the slow burn of the bar encounter to the explosive action to the tense motel standoff. The ending maintains the unresolved tension requested in the prompt without forcing artificial resolution. Both characters remain true to their established personalities - neither backing down completely nor escalating to actual violence. The mutual respect and attraction remain implicit rather than stated. Some minor weaknesses include occasional repetitive sentence structures and a few moments where the dialogue could be more distinctive. The piece also relies heavily on external action to drive the plot forward rather than deeper character exploration, though this fits the noir genre conventions. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Folk horror: Útiseta — Score: 87.0
Prompt:
Folk Horror: Útiseta Write a chapter set in rural northern Sweden in the mid-1990s. Linnea, a teenage girl from Stockholm, has reluctantly moved to a small village with her parents after her father's job transfer. She's befriended Astrid, a local girl who claims her grandmother taught her ancient practices passed down through generations. Astrid has been introducing Linnea to "midnight games" that involve old rituals, convincing her they can use them to learn classmates' secrets or influence their behavior. Tonight, Astrid has convinced Linnea to participate in Útiseta—sitting out on an ancient burial mound at midnight to "receive knowledge from beyond." The chapter focuses on this single midnight excursion, the ritualistic preparations, Astrid's matter-of-fact instructions that mix teenage rebellion with eerily specific traditional elements, and the mounting tension as Linnea becomes increasingly uncertain whether they're simply scaring themselves or if something is actually responding to their presence. The narrative should maintain ambiguity about whether the strange occurrences are supernatural or psychological, while establishing the dangerous line they're crossing by treating ancient practices as entertainment. First person, past tense, told as Linnea's recollection of events in the present day (though this device should be largely transparent). 1000 words. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing I remember the cold first. Not the sharp winter cold Stockholm had, the kind that glittered on sidewalks and made the air taste metallic, but a slow, creeping cold that came up from the ground itself. Northern cold. Soil cold. It seeped through the seat of my jeans as soon as I sat down. “Don’t move once you start,” Astrid said. “That’s important.” She said everything that way—like she was reminding me how to play a board game we both already knew. Matter‑of‑fact. No drama. The burial mound was just a hump in the field during the day. I’d passed it on the school bus without noticing it more than once. But at night it looked deliberate. Like something pushed up from beneath. “Are you sure people are buried here?” I whispered. “Of course,” Astrid said. She crouched beside me, arranging things between us in the grass. A small candle in a jam jar. A piece of bread. A coin. Her grandmother’s knife, though she had promised me earlier we wouldn’t be cutting anything tonight. “It’s called Útiseta,” she said. “You sit out. You wait. Eventually something answers.” “What kind of something?” She shrugged. “Depends what you ask.” The candle flame shivered in the wind, throwing our shadows across the uneven mound. Beyond the field the forest pressed in, black and dense, the spruce trees swallowing what little moonlight there was. I had agreed to this two days earlier in the school cafeteria, when everything had felt like a joke. Astrid had already shown me other “games.” We’d buried a note with someone’s name under a stone and waited to see who texted us first. We’d whispered questions into a glass of water and left it on the windowsill overnight. Once we’d sat cross‑legged in her room asking who in our class had a crush on who, and Astrid had insisted the candle flicker meant yes. It had all felt like the kind of nonsense girls do when they’re bored. But this was different. For one thing, we weren’t in her warm bedroom. And for another, Astrid kept doing small things that felt too specific to be invented. “Take your shoes off,” she said. “What?” “You have to touch the ground.” “It’s freezing.” “Exactly.” I stared at her. She was serious. Astrid had grown up here her whole life. Her family owned a yellow wooden house near the edge of the forest, and her grandmother still lived there too, silent and thin and always watching when I came over. “My mormor taught me,” Astrid had said once. “These things used to be normal.” At the time I thought she meant like old wives’ tales. Now, sitting on what might actually be someone’s grave, I wasn’t so sure. I sighed and pulled off my sneakers. The grass was wet. The cold shot through my feet immediately, like stepping into icy water. Astrid nodded approvingly. “Good. Now face north.” “How do you even know which way that is?” She pointed past the mound. “The mountain.” I twisted until I was facing the dark bulk on the horizon. “Now what?” I asked. “Now you sit.” “That’s it?” “Mostly.” She handed me the coin. “Hold this in your mouth.” “What?” “Just for a while.” “This is stupid.” “Do you want to know things or not?” I rolled my eyes but took the coin. It tasted like metal and old pockets. Astrid lit the candle. The flame steadied inside the jar, glowing amber against the grass. “Okay,” she said quietly. “From now on we don’t talk unless it’s part of the question.” “What question?” “You’ll know.” She settled beside me on the mound, cross‑legged, hands resting on her knees. For a while nothing happened. The field stretched silent around us. Somewhere far off, water moved—maybe the river. A dog barked once and then stopped. I tried to keep track of time, but without my phone it dissolved quickly. The coin warmed slowly under my tongue. My feet were numb. At some point I realized I was holding my breath. I let it out carefully. Astrid hadn’t moved. Her eyes were half‑closed, like she was listening to something distant. I leaned slightly toward her. “Astrid—” She shook her head sharply. Right. No talking. The wind shifted. The forest made a soft, restless sound. Branches rubbing together. I told myself that was all it was. Still, the mound beneath us felt strangely hollow. Not literally hollow. Just… present. Like sitting on someone’s chest. My mind wandered back to school. Astrid had said this could reveal secrets. “Sometimes you learn who’s lying about you,” she’d told me earlier that evening as we biked out here. “Sometimes you learn what someone’s hiding.” “And sometimes?” She had smiled. “Sometimes something else answers.” At the time I assumed she was joking. Now the words sat in my stomach like stones. The candle flickered. I glanced at it. The flame bent sideways though the air around us had gone still. Astrid opened her eyes. Slowly, she turned her head toward the forest. I followed her gaze. Nothing. Just trees. Then— From very far away— A bell rang. One clear tone. It carried across the fields, thin but unmistakable. The church. I knew the sound instantly. The white wooden church stood by the lake on the other side of the village. But there was no service tonight. The bell never rang at midnight. Astrid’s voice came out barely above a whisper. “That means it heard.” I almost spit out the coin. “What heard?” “Shh.” Her hand closed around my wrist, cold and tight. “Ask,” she murmured. My heart thudded painfully. “Ask what?” “Anything.” I stared at the forest. The darkness between the trees looked deeper than it should. “You said secrets,” I whispered. “That’s one way.” The candle flame stretched tall, then shrank. For a second the wind returned, brushing over the grass like fingers. I thought about the girls at school who laughed when I spoke. About the way everyone here already knew each other’s families and histories and winter stories. I thought about wanting something—anything—that gave me an edge. My mouth felt dry around the coin. I took it out. The metal was warm now. “What if nothing happens?” I said. Astrid didn’t look at me. “Then we go home.” “And if something does?” She considered that. “Then we listen carefully.” I swallowed. The ground beneath us seemed to settle slightly, the way earth does after a heavy step. But neither of us had moved. I told myself it was just my imagination. Just nerves. Still, I leaned closer to the candle. And very quietly, feeling suddenly foolish, I asked the darkness beyond the mound: “Who here lies about me?” For a long moment there was nothing. Just the faint hiss of the candle. Then the wind returned. Not strong. But deliberate. It moved across the mound from the direction of the forest, passing over us like breath. Astrid’s grip tightened around my wrist. “Don’t stand up,” she whispered urgently. “I wasn’t—” “Whatever happens, don’t stand up.” The grass around the candle stirred. The flame bent low, almost horizontal. And beneath us, so faint I might have imagined it, the ground gave a soft, muffled knock. Once. From somewhere inside the mound. :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully creating a folk horror atmosphere set in 1990s rural Sweden. The author effectively employs the first-person retrospective narration while keeping the framing device largely transparent, as requested. The 1000-word length is respected, and the focus remains tightly on the single midnight excursion. The characterization is nuanced and believable. Linnea emerges as a convincing teenage protagonist - skeptical yet curious, caught between urban sophistication and rural mysticism. Her internal conflict between dismissing the ritual as "nonsense girls do when they're bored" and her growing unease feels authentic. Astrid is particularly well-crafted, with her matter-of-fact delivery of supernatural instructions creating an unsettling contrast that drives the horror effectively. The prose quality is consistently high, with elegant, controlled language that avoids purple prose while maintaining atmospheric richness. Sentences like "Northern cold. Soil cold" and "The darkness between the trees looked deeper than it should" demonstrate skillful economy of language. The author shows excellent restraint, building tension through accumulation of small, specific details rather than overwrought description. The piece excels at maintaining the crucial ambiguity between supernatural and psychological explanations. The bell ringing could be coincidence, the ground settling could be imagination, yet the specificity of Astrid's knowledge and the ritualistic elements suggest deeper forces at work. This ambiguity is central to effective folk horror and is handled with sophistication. The imagery is vivid and grounded in sensory detail - the cold seeping through jeans, the taste of the metal coin, the candle flame's behavior. These concrete details anchor the supernatural elements in physical reality, making the uncanny more effective. The dialogue serves multiple purposes efficiently, revealing character while advancing plot and building atmosphere. Astrid's casual delivery of supernatural instructions ("Don't move once you start") contrasts effectively with Linnea's more naturalistic responses. The pacing builds tension methodically without meandering, moving from setup through ritual preparation to the climactic supernatural contact. The ending strikes an appropriate note of unresolved tension without forced positivity or unearned transformation. Minor weaknesses include some predictable elements in the setup and a few moments where the prose could be tighter, but these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 17
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 18.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 17.0, Emotionally Complex: 16.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 4.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6.0, Amateurish: 3.0, Purple Prose: 2.0, Overwrought: 3.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 18.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 17.0
+ Physical-spatial: Therapeutic Boundaries — Score: 88.7
Prompt:
**"Therapeutic Boundaries"** Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of a former professional athlete undergoing mandatory physical therapy after a career-destroying injury, harboring resentment toward recovery itself. Their physiotherapist is not only unnervingly attractive but also a former training partner from their competitive days who has successfully transitioned to a new career while the protagonist's identity remains shattered. We'll lean into the sexual tension of the scene by having her be attracted to him, but both of them attempting to maintain mental distance while being in physical contact as he shows her stretches & movements & manipulates her body, navigating the thresholds of pain. This scene MUST maintain complete physical coherence—every movement, stretch, and manipulation must be anatomically precise and spatially consistent. Paint an exceptionally vivid sensory picture through detailed attention to pain thresholds, involuntary responses to touch. Capture the suppressed, flaring tension of physical proximity, touch, and the controlled infliction of uncomfortable movements. Physical clarity is essential — readers must understand exactly what is happening with the body during each therapeutic technique. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The first thing I notice every session is the diagram. It hangs on the wall directly above the treatment table, a laminated figure with its skin peeled away, every muscle rendered in glossy reds and striated pinks. Deltoid. Sartorius. Vastus medialis. A body reduced to parts and angles and leverage. No crowd noise. No finish lines. No identity. Just tissue. “Lie back,” she says. I already am, but I slide farther onto the table anyway, shoulders flattening against the vinyl. The paper sheet crackles under my weight. The clinic smells like disinfectant and something faintly minty. Maya stands at the foot of the table, tablet tucked under one arm. Same dark hair she had when we trained together years ago, though now it’s tied back in a neat knot instead of a sweat-soaked ponytail. Same long lines through the legs. She used to beat me in interval sessions. Now she fixes broken people. “Right knee today,” she says. “Lucky me.” Her mouth tilts like she’s deciding whether to laugh. She doesn’t. “Bend it.” I slide my heel toward my hips. The knee stops early, like it hits an invisible wall. “That’s as far as it goes,” I say. She steps closer. Up close, I can see the faint freckles across the bridge of her nose. My brain offers that detail for no useful reason. “Let me see.” Her hands slide under my heel and calf. Her palms are warm and dry. She lifts my leg slightly, adjusting the angle until my thigh is vertical. The contact is clinical. Efficient. My nervous system does not care. “Relax,” she says. “I am relaxed.” “You’re guarding.” “Because it hurts.” “That’s the point.” She shifts one hand behind my ankle, the other just below my knee. Then she slowly pushes my heel toward my body. The joint tightens instantly. Scar tissue feels like someone cinching a belt inside your knee. “That’s seventy-five degrees,” she murmurs, glancing at a small goniometer she’s braced along the joint. “Feels like a hundred.” “Breathe.” I inhale. The ceiling tiles blur slightly. She presses again. The pain arrives sharp and electric, a wire pulled tight through the center of the knee. My quadriceps jump. “There,” she says quietly. “That reflex.” “I didn’t invite it.” “You don’t have to.” Her thumb presses gently into the tendon just above my kneecap. “Quad’s trying to take over.” “Because the hamstring’s useless.” “It’s healing.” “It’s retired.” She doesn’t answer. Instead she holds the stretch exactly where the discomfort blooms but hasn’t tipped into something worse. There’s an art to it. I remember that from training—the exact edge where effort becomes damage. Her face is closer now. Concentrating. When we trained together, she had this same expression during starts. Focused like the rest of the world had been muted. “Okay,” she says softly. “Two more degrees.” “Two degrees can go to hell.” She smiles despite herself. “Ready?” “No.” She pushes anyway. The sensation is slow, deliberate. Heel inching closer to my hip. The knee joint compressing. A deep, dragging burn along the back of the thigh where the hamstring tendon had ripped itself off the bone eight months ago. My hands curl against the table. “Don’t hold your breath,” she says. “I’m considering passing out.” “That’s inefficient.” Another fraction of movement. My entire leg trembles. “Good,” she murmurs. “Define good.” “You’re not stopping me.” That’s when I realize her hip is lightly braced against the side of the table for leverage. Her forearm rests along my shin to stabilize the tibia. We are very close. Professional close. But close. I stare at the muscle diagram above her head. The illustrated hamstrings fan down the back of the thigh like ropes. Semitendinosus. Semimembranosus. Biceps femoris. Mine feel like frayed cables. “Okay,” she says after a long moment, easing the pressure. “Slow extension.” She guides my heel away, straightening the knee again. Relief floods the joint so fast it almost makes me dizzy. “That,” I mutter, “was torture.” “That was progress.” She sets my leg down carefully. Then she moves to the side of the table. “Roll onto your left side.” I comply. The paper sheet crackles again. My injured leg ends up on top. She steps behind me. “Top knee bent ninety degrees,” she says, guiding it forward with one hand. “Bottom leg straight.” Her palm settles on my hip. “Clamshells?” “Modified.” Her fingers slide slightly to find the bony ridge of the pelvis. “Don’t roll backward,” she says. “I’ll stabilize.” Her forearm presses gently along my waist while the other hand cups the outside of my knee. “Now lift.” I rotate the knee upward. The movement is small. Maybe a foot of separation between my knees. My glute medius lights up like someone flipped a switch. “Hold.” The muscle shakes instantly. She leans in slightly, watching the alignment. “You’re compensating.” “How.” “Pelvis is drifting.” Her hand tightens subtly against my hip, anchoring me. The pressure is firm, grounding. “Again,” she says. I lower the knee. Lift it again. The burn spreads along the side of my hip. “Five reps,” she says. “Feels like fifty.” “You used to do hill sprints for fun.” “Past life.” Her breath brushes my shoulder when she leans closer to check the angle. “Three more.” Her fingers adjust my knee slightly outward. The correction sends a sharper line of effort through the muscle. “Right there,” she says. “That’s the one.” “Glad someone’s enjoying this.” She snorts softly. When I finish the fifth rep, my leg drops. “Pathetic,” I say. “Rebuilding,” she corrects. There’s a pause. Her hand is still resting on my hip. Neither of us moves for a second too long. I can feel the warmth of her palm through the thin fabric of my shorts. My body is hyper-aware of every point of contact—the table under my ribs, the steady pressure of her hand, the faint scent of her shampoo. We used to stand shoulder to shoulder on starting lines. Now she measures angles in my ruined knee. She clears her throat and steps back. “Sit up,” she says. I swing my legs off the table slowly. The musculature diagram looms above us. A perfect body without scars. “Same time Thursday,” she says, tapping something into her tablet. “Can’t wait.” She glances up at me. There’s that old competitive spark in her eyes. “You’ll run again,” she says. I laugh once. But my knee still feels the echo of her hands guiding it past the place I thought it stopped. And annoyingly— it moved. :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution in several key areas while maintaining the delicate balance required by the prompt's complex emotional and physical dynamics. The narrative successfully establishes the protagonist's bitter resentment toward both their situation and the recovery process, while Maya emerges as a fully realized character rather than merely an object of attraction or professional authority. The anatomical precision requested is expertly handled. The writer demonstrates clear understanding of physical therapy techniques, joint mechanics, and muscle groups. Details like the goniometer measurement at seventy-five degrees, the specific muscle names (vastus medialis, semitendinosus), and the precise description of hamstring reattachment surgery create authentic medical context. The physical movements are spatially coherent throughout - readers can visualize exactly how bodies are positioned during each exercise. The sexual tension is masterfully understated. Rather than relying on overwrought descriptions, the writer uses clinical proximity and professional touch to create genuine tension. Moments like Maya's hip braced against the table for leverage, or the pause where her hand remains on the protagonist's hip "a second too long," feel natural and earned rather than manufactured. The voice remains consistently bitter and sardonic throughout, with the protagonist's resentment manifesting in sharp, economical dialogue and internal observations. Lines like "Two degrees can go to hell" and "Define good" capture the character's resistance without becoming cartoonish. The sensory details are particularly strong - the crackle of paper sheets, the smell of disinfectant and mint, the specific quality of scar tissue pain described as "someone cinching a belt inside your knee." These concrete details ground the reader in the clinical setting while maintaining the emotional undercurrents. The ending strikes an appropriate note - neither falsely optimistic nor completely despairing. The protagonist's grudging acknowledgment that the knee "moved" provides a subtle shift without betraying the established character resistance or suggesting unrealistic transformation. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward being slightly too polished for the bitter narrator's voice, and a few instances where the medical terminology, while accurate, slightly interrupts the narrative flow. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 17 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 18 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
Scores: Adherence to Instructions: 19.0, Believable Character Actions: 18.0, Nuanced Characters: 17.0, Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18.0, Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 19.0, Elegant Prose: 17.0, Emotionally Engaging: 18.0, Emotionally Complex: 18.0, Coherent: 19.0, Meandering: 2.0, Weak Dialogue: 3.0, Tell-Don't-Show: 3.0, Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4.0, Amateurish: 2.0, Purple Prose: 3.0, Overwrought: 2.0, Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2.0, Unearned Transformations: 2.0, Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17.0, Sentences Flow Naturally: 17.0, Overall Reader Engagement: 18.0, Overall Impression: 18.0
+ Historical fiction: High Seas and Low Vices — Score: 83.0
Prompt:
High Seas and Low Vices: British Naval Officer in the Opium Trade Character: Lieutenant James Merrick, a high-ranking officer in the British Royal Navy stationed in Canton, China in the 1830s. Once promising and decorated, now struggling with a severe opium addiction he hides from his superiors. Synopsis: Write a chapter following Lieutenant Merrick during a single day as he shirks his official naval duties in what has become a daily need to satiate the dragon. Told primarily in dialogue with the characters he interacts with. Focus on brief slices of his journey through the shadowy underworld of Canton: interactions with acquaintances, negotiations with local dealers, schemes and unsavoury jobs taken to fund his habit. Writing Task: Use rich sensory details to describe a day in the life of James Merrick. Write for a real sense of time & place while avoiding cliche. First person, past tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The river fog had not yet burned away when I slipped down the gangway and onto the Canton mud. The Pearl smelled of brine, dung, and hot iron from the smiths already hammering somewhere upriver. I told the watchman I was bound for the factory offices. He pretended to believe me. My hands were already trembling. I pressed the small jade figure in my pocket between thumb and forefinger—cool, smooth, reassuring as a rosary bead. A little horse, carved with absurd delicacy. I rubbed its worn flank while the craving moved through me like fever. “Lieutenant Merrick,” someone called. I nearly turned back to the ship out of reflex. Instead I found Mr. Bell, a Scottish merchant agent, standing beside a stack of tea chests. “Thought you were on duty this morning,” he said. “So did I.” He squinted at me. “You look like a ghost that’s been rained on.” “Didn’t sleep.” Bell lowered his voice. “You here on business?” “Of a sort.” His mouth twitched with amusement. “Then you’ll want the lower street. Police were through here last night.” He leaned closer. “And Merrick—try not to die before paying your debts.” “I’ve no intention of dying,” I said. But the lie tasted sour. I left him laughing and moved deeper into the narrow streets behind the foreign factories. Canton woke slowly but noisily. Vendors shouted over baskets of fish that still twitched. Someone roasted duck; the scent of fat and charred skin made my stomach twist. My hands were shaking harder now. The jade horse slid through my fingers. I turned down an alley where laundry hung like damp sails between buildings. A boy sitting on a crate looked up. “Morning, Lieutenant.” “Morning, Lin.” He could not have been older than twelve. He chewed a sugarcane stick and eyed me with bored familiarity. “You late today.” “Am I?” “Master Chen said sunrise.” “Sunrise came early.” Lin shrugged and hopped down. “You owe money,” he said matter-of-factly. “I’m painfully aware.” He led me through a wooden door and up a crooked stair that smelled of smoke and fermented rice. At the top sat Master Chen, cross-legged beside a low table. He was weighing silver. “Lieutenant Merrick,” Chen said without looking up. “Master Chen.” “You bring payment?” “Not yet.” Chen slid the scale weights aside and finally met my eyes. “You still smoke.” “Occasionally.” “You still owe.” “That part I remember clearly.” Lin snorted. Chen folded his hands into his sleeves. “Last time you promise money tomorrow. Today you bring no money. Why should I help you?” The craving tightened around my spine like a screw. My skin prickled. I rubbed the jade horse until the edges dug into my thumb. “I can be useful,” I said. Chen tilted his head. “You are already useful. British officer who buys opium.” “I meant more useful than that.” He considered. “What do you offer?” “There’s a cutter arriving tonight from Lintin Island. Opium cargo.” I paused. “Royal Navy inspection scheduled at dusk.” Chen’s eyebrow lifted. “And?” “And the inspection party will be delayed if the officer responsible happens to misplace the paperwork.” Lin laughed outright. “You sell your own navy now?” “I misplace paperwork,” I corrected. Chen studied me quietly. “You want pipe now,” he said. I swallowed. “Yes.” “You do work first.” “What work?” He gestured to a wooden crate near the wall. “Deliver.” “To whom?” “Dock warehouse. Man named Gao.” “What’s inside?” “Medicine.” Lin grinned. “Always medicine.” My legs felt watery. “I’ll deliver your medicine,” I said. Chen nodded to the crate. “Then come back. We see about your pipe.” Lin and I carried the crate down the stairs together. “You getting worse,” he said. “Thank you for the medical opinion.” “You shake.” “I’m cold.” “It’s thirty degrees.” I said nothing. Outside, the streets had grown crowded. Porters shouted, carts rattled over stone, and somewhere a musician played a reedy flute that pierced my skull like a needle. We reached the dock warehouse. A man with a scar across his cheek stood by the door. “Gao?” I asked. He nodded. Lin pointed to the crate. “Medicine.” Gao pried open the lid with a knife. Inside lay tightly packed opium bricks wrapped in oilcloth. He glanced at me. “Royal Navy?” “Unfortunately.” “You bring trouble?” “Not today.” He tossed Lin a coin and shut the crate again. “Tell Chen shipment safe.” Lin pocketed the coin and ran off immediately. I stayed. Gao watched me a moment. “You sick?” he said. “I’ve had better mornings.” He leaned against the door. “Many English smoke now.” “Empire of habits.” “You want pipe?” he asked. The question landed in my chest like salvation. “Yes.” He jerked his head toward the back room. Inside the air hung thick with the sweet, dark perfume of cooked opium. Lamps glowed low. Two sailors already lay on bamboo couches, eyes half closed. My hands nearly stopped shaking just from the smell. Gao prepared the pipe with practiced calm. “You pay later,” he said. “I always do.” “That is not what Chen says.” “Chen is a pessimist.” He handed me the pipe. The first breath filled my lungs like warm velvet. The world softened immediately, edges melting. The trembling ebbed. I sank back against the bamboo mat. Gao watched the smoke curl toward the ceiling. “You navy men bring war soon,” he said. “Do we?” “Government angry about trade.” “Our government tends to get angry when it can’t tax something.” “You believe in this trade?” he asked. I rolled the jade horse across my knuckles, slow now, calm. “Belief has nothing to do with it.” Another breath of smoke. Outside the room someone laughed loudly. “What do you believe?” Gao said. I exhaled and watched the smoke drift away like fog off the Pearl River. “I believe,” I said slowly, “that if I miss tonight’s inspection, a captain will shout at me.” “Yes.” “And if I miss this pipe, my bones will crawl out of my skin.” Gao nodded. “So which problem is bigger?” I smiled faintly. “That,” I said, drawing in another breath, “is the empire’s great philosophical question.” :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric writing within the historical setting of 1830s Canton. The author successfully creates a vivid sense of place through sensory details like "The Pearl smelled of brine, dung, and hot iron" and "the sweet, dark perfume of cooked opium." The dialogue feels natural and period-appropriate, with characters speaking in distinct voices that reveal their relationships and social positions. The characterization of Lieutenant Merrick is particularly well-handled. His addiction is portrayed with psychological authenticity - the physical symptoms, the rationalization, the gradual moral compromise. The jade horse serves as an effective recurring motif, representing both his deteriorating state and his attempt to maintain some semblance of control. His interactions reveal a man who retains his wit and intelligence even as he descends into dependency. The dialogue-heavy structure works well, allowing character relationships to emerge naturally. The exchanges between Merrick and various figures - Bell, Lin, Chen, and Gao - each serve different narrative functions while maintaining believable speech patterns. The conversations reveal backstory and advance plot without feeling expository. The piece effectively captures the moral ambiguity of the historical period without heavy-handed commentary. The final philosophical exchange about competing problems (missing inspection vs. missing the pipe) encapsulates the protagonist's dilemma elegantly. However, the piece occasionally relies on somewhat predictable beats in the addiction narrative - the trembling hands, the mounting desperation, the moral compromise for drugs. While executed competently, these elements don't surprise. The ending, while thematically appropriate, doesn't offer much resolution or insight beyond what's already been established. The prose is generally clean and effective, though it sometimes veers toward being overly polished in a way that slightly distances the reader from Merrick's degraded state. Some moments could benefit from more rawness to match the character's condition. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Humour: Sisyphus Gives Advice — Score: 89.1
Prompt:
Sisyphus Gives Advice Write a comedic scene in the style of Terry Pratchett featuring Sisyphus, who has started an advice column while continuing his eternal boulder-pushing. Rather than being bitter about his punishment, Sisyphus has developed a comfortably zen emotional intelligence and wisdom from his centuries of repetitive labor. Not in the cliche ways you might expect -- he's actually very grounded, warm & connected with the everyday human plight. He dictates responses to "Crow," his feathered familiar who serves as both scribe and mail carrier. The piece should include Sisyphus thoughtfully answering several advice-seekers' letters about everyday problems (relationship troubles, work-life balance, dealing with difficult people, life purpose doubts) while periodically returning to his boulder-pushing duties. His advice should be surprisingly insightful and practical. The humor should emerge naturally from the messages he receives, from mundane to risque. Sisyphus should genuinely enjoy helping others, finding meaning in this new pursuit while maintaining his boulder routine. The humour should be *understated and not forced*. Contrary to expectation, almost none of his advice involves boulder rolling metaphors (though he does sneak *one* subtle in there), nor a treatise on stoicism. We are also going against the obvious fish-out-of-water trope; in fact, he's surprisingly a natural at this. In all of this , it's important to *show, don't tell*. The format should be largely epistolary, with the occasional interjection for scene setting. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The hill was quiet except for the steady, patient sound of a man pushing a boulder and a crow complaining about handwriting. “Crow,” said Sisyphus, leaning his shoulder into the stone with the easy familiarity of someone who had done this more times than the concept of counting could reasonably support, “are you ready?” Crow dipped his quill in ink. “Ready is a strong word. But I am perched.” The boulder moved a little further uphill with the dignified stubbornness of something that knew exactly how this was going to end. “First letter,” Crow said, rustling parchment. “From—ah—*‘Confused in Corinth.’*” “Go on.” Crow cleared his throat. “Dear Sisyphus, My partner and I argue constantly about household chores. They say I never clean the dishes. I say they never appreciate the things I do clean. Last night we had a three-hour argument about a spoon. What should I do?” Sisyphus pushed a little harder. The boulder rolled three feet. He considered. “Dictate,” he said. Crow poised the quill. “Dear Confused in Corinth, First: three hours is too long to argue about a spoon. Even if it is a very nice spoon. Most arguments about chores are not about chores. They are about feeling unseen. When people say ‘You never wash the dishes,’ what they usually mean is ‘I would like some help and I’m tired of asking for it.’ Try this experiment: for two weeks, do the dishes before your partner notices them. Don’t announce it. Don’t sigh meaningfully. Just do them. Meanwhile, once a day, thank your partner for something specific they did. People are astonishingly willing to cooperate once they feel noticed. If, after two weeks, the spoon is still a battlefield, then you may have discovered that the problem was never the spoon. Yours, Sisyphus” Crow scribbled. Then he added a small note in the margin. *[Editor’s note: If the spoon is cursed, please write again.]* “Next,” Crow said. The boulder reached a familiar ridge in the hill. Sisyphus paused, rested his hands on it, and wiped his forehead with a cloth. Crow shuffled another letter. “Ah. Workplace misery. Always popular.” He read. “Dear Sisyphus, My boss is impossible. He micromanages everything I do, sends emails at midnight, and once corrected my stapling technique. I feel like I'm slowly going insane. How do I deal with someone like this?” Sisyphus nodded slowly. “Dictate.” Crow prepared. “Dear Slowly Going Insane, There are three types of difficult bosses. The first is the one who does not trust you. The second is the one who does not trust themselves. The third is the one who simply enjoys control. You can handle the first two. For a week, send your boss short updates before they ask for them. ‘I’ve completed X. Next I’ll begin Y.’ People who micromanage often calm down once they see movement. If they correct your stapling again, thank them sincerely and ask them to show you their preferred method. People who crave authority tend to feel full after being fed small, harmless opportunities to demonstrate it. If none of this helps, begin looking quietly for another job. Life is long, but it should not feel that way between 9 and 5. Yours, Sisyphus” Crow added a note. *[Editor’s note: The correct stapling technique is “any technique that involves a staple.”]* Sisyphus leaned back into the boulder and pushed again. It rolled upward with patient inevitability. Crow watched him for a moment. “You know,” the crow said, “for someone cursed by the gods, you’re remarkably upbeat.” “I have a hobby now,” said Sisyphus. “Hmm.” Another parchment. “Ah! Spicy.” Crow read aloud. “Dear Sisyphus, My neighbor’s wife keeps flirting with me. At first I thought I imagined it, but yesterday she baked me honey cakes and winked. Her husband is a large man who wrestles goats recreationally. What is the wisest course of action?” Sisyphus pushed the boulder another few steps. “Dictate.” Crow smirked. “Dear Concerned Neighbor, When people flirt, they are usually looking for attention, excitement, or a small story to brighten their day. You do not have to become the story. Be friendly. Be polite. But introduce her—frequently—to other people. ‘Have you met my friend Thales? He’s fascinating about irrigation canals.’ Attention tends to flow toward the nearest available audience. Also, mention her husband often, and always in admiring terms. ‘Your husband must be very strong to wrestle goats.’ Flirtation thrives on secrecy. It tends to wither under cheerful sunlight. Yours, Sisyphus” Crow added: *[Editor’s note: Never compete with a man who wrestles goats. They are surprisingly agile.]* The boulder crept higher. Wind stirred the grass. Crow opened another letter. “This one smells philosophical,” he said suspiciously. He read. “Dear Sisyphus, Everyone else seems to know what they’re doing with their lives. Careers, families, ambitions. I wake up most mornings wondering what the point of anything is. How do you find purpose?” Sisyphus stopped pushing. For a while he said nothing. The boulder waited. Finally he nodded. “Dictate.” Crow leaned closer. “Dear Wondering, Most people who look like they have everything figured out are simply walking briskly in a direction they chose yesterday. Purpose is rarely discovered like buried treasure. It is more often assembled from smaller pieces. Try three things. First: help someone with a problem that is not your own. It interrupts the echo chamber. Second: build something small that did not exist yesterday. A meal, a shelf, a garden bed. The brain enjoys evidence of forward motion. Third: keep a short list of things that make an ordinary day slightly better. Good bread. Sunlight on a wall. A conversation that lingers. If you collect enough of these days, they eventually resemble a life. Yours, Sisyphus” Crow stared at the parchment. “Hm,” he said. Then he added in the margin: *[Editor’s note: The author has strong feelings about good bread.]* A moment passed. Then Crow shuffled the final letter. “Oh, this one’s excellent.” He read. “Dear Sisyphus, My family expects me to visit every festival, attend every gathering, and respond to every message immediately. I love them, but it’s exhausting. How do I set boundaries without causing a war?” Sisyphus chuckled softly. “Ah,” he said. “Families.” The boulder rolled another foot uphill. “Dictate.” “Dear Exhausted, Most families operate on momentum. If you attend every gathering, they assume you always will. If you answer every message immediately, they assume you are always available. Change momentum gently. Miss one event and send a kind message instead. Reply a few hours later instead of instantly. People adjust faster than you think, especially if you remain warm. Boundaries work best when they are quiet and consistent. Also remember: you are allowed to build a life that fits you, not just one that fits everyone else. Think of it like pushing a large stone uphill—progress happens slowly, but direction matters more than speed. Yours, Sisyphus” Crow froze. “You did it,” he said. “Did what?” “You used the boulder.” Sisyphus smiled. “One per column,” he said. The boulder reached the summit. They both watched as it paused for a thoughtful moment… and then rolled back down the hill. Crow sighed. “I’ll go collect tomorrow’s mail.” He spread his wings. Below them, letters waited in villages and cities and kitchens and quiet rooms where people wondered small, human things. Sisyphus stretched his shoulders and began walking downhill after the boulder. “Bring extra parchment,” he called. Crow cawed back. *[Editor’s note: Send bread.]* :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of both the prompt requirements and Terry Pratchett's distinctive style. The author successfully creates an advice column format that feels authentic while maintaining the whimsical, understated humor characteristic of Pratchett's work. The characterization of Sisyphus is particularly well-executed. Rather than falling into obvious tropes about eternal punishment or stoicism, the author presents him as genuinely wise and grounded, with his advice feeling practical and earned through experience. The relationship between Sisyphus and Crow provides excellent comedic relief through their natural banter and Crow's editorial notes, which add layers of humor without feeling forced. The epistolary format works effectively, with each letter representing realistic human problems. The advice given is genuinely insightful - particularly the responses about household arguments being about feeling unseen, and the nuanced approach to dealing with difficult bosses. The author shows restraint in avoiding heavy-handed boulder metaphors, saving the single reference for a well-timed moment that Crow immediately calls out. The prose style captures Pratchett's voice admirably, with dry observations like "the boulder moved with the dignified stubbornness of something that knew exactly how this was going to end" and "Most people who look like they have everything figured out are simply walking briskly in a direction they chose yesterday." The humor emerges naturally from character interactions and situational irony rather than forced jokes. The piece maintains excellent pacing, balancing the advice-giving with scene-setting moments of boulder-pushing. The ending feels organic, with Sisyphus's contentment in his new purpose feeling earned rather than artificially positive. The writing demonstrates strong technical control, with varied sentence structures and natural dialogue flow. Minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the wisdom feels slightly too polished for casual advice, and some descriptions could be more vivid. However, these are minor quibbles with an otherwise accomplished piece that successfully fulfills all the prompt requirements while creating genuinely engaging content. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 19 Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 19 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 19 Meandering: 2 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 3 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 2 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Sentences Flow Naturally: 18 Overall Reader Engagement: 18 Overall Impression: 18
