+ Iteration 2 — Avg Score: 81.5
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 81.3
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
Arthur Neegan adjusted his collar for perhaps the twelfth time since the transfer vessel had left Mars orbit. The garment resisted him with quiet dignity. It was a high-collared formal coat of dark synthweave, clearly designed by someone who had never spent a day in a pressure rig. The sleeves tugged when he moved his arms, and the collar seemed intent on sawing its way across the back of his neck. He tried sitting still. Outside the observation port, Mars had dwindled to a dull rust-colored sphere, veiled in thin cloud and orbital dust. Freighters drifted across the black like patient insects. Somewhere among them was the ore hauler that should have been carrying his latest Azra sample to the testing lab. Instead, Arthur Neegan was on a diplomatic courier vessel owned—if the insignia meant anything—by the Antares Diplomatic Corps. He adjusted the collar again. “It's not going to get more comfortable,” said a voice behind him. Arthur turned. The speaker was one of the soldiers who had arrived at his homestead four hours earlier—unannounced, heavily armed, and impeccably polite. The man wore a dark uniform marked with the stylized starburst of Antares. The insignia had a reputation that traveled faster than their ships. Arthur said, “You people could have warned me about the wardrobe.” The soldier shrugged. “Protocol.” “Protocol,” Arthur repeated. “For kidnapping?” The soldier smiled faintly. “We prefer the term invitation.” “You arrived with six rifles.” “Standard diplomatic courtesy.” Arthur gave a dry laugh and turned back to the port. Mars hung there like a rusted coin. “Who am I meeting?” he asked. “I'm not authorized to say.” “Then authorize yourself.” The soldier folded his arms. “You discovered something valuable, Mr. Neegan.” “I discovered a rock.” “A rock composed of Azra in concentrations no one believed possible.” Arthur did not reply. Silence stretched between them, filled only by the quiet hum of the vessel’s fusion drive. Finally Arthur said, “My survey results weren’t public yet.” “No,” said the soldier. “They weren’t.” Arthur turned slowly. “That means someone either broke into my lab records,” he said, “or intercepted a private corporate transmission.” The soldier said nothing. Arthur studied him. “Which corporation do you work for?” Arthur asked. “I don’t.” “That’s funny,” Arthur said. “Everyone works for someone.” The soldier’s smile returned, faint and professional. “Yes,” he said. “That’s generally true.” The ship shuddered gently. A soft tone sounded. “Approach complete,” said a distant automated voice. Arthur looked back out the port. The transfer vessel was nearing a larger craft now sliding into view from behind the curve of the planet’s shadow. Arthur frowned. “That’s not Antares registry.” The ship outside was enormous—sleek and black, its hull broken by luminous bands of engine shielding. It had the unmistakable profile of a deep-system diplomatic cruiser. But the markings were wrong. There were none. Arthur stood. “You people came all the way from the Belt for a mining engineer?” “Please remain seated,” said the soldier. Arthur ignored him. “You could have just sent a message.” “Messages can be intercepted.” “And hauling me off-world isn’t suspicious?” “Less than you might think.” Arthur let out a breath. “Let me guess,” he said. “My claim has suddenly become very popular.” The soldier did not answer. Arthur nodded slowly. “That means the test results leaked already.” Still no answer. Arthur laughed softly. “That’s impressive,” he said. “It took less than twelve hours.” “You underestimate the importance of Azra.” Arthur turned back toward the window. “No,” he said quietly. “I don’t.” The docking clamps engaged with a solid metallic thud. The soldier straightened. “Mr. Neegan,” he said, “we’ve arrived.” — The diplomatic cruiser’s interior was absurdly spacious. Arthur noticed it immediately. Ships designed for efficiency did not waste volume. Freight carriers packed their corridors tight as intestines. Even military vessels economized. But this ship had wide passageways, polished alloy floors, and lighting designed to be aesthetically pleasing rather than merely functional. Someone had built this ship to impress. Arthur was escorted down a long corridor by the same soldier and two others. They stopped at a circular door. It opened silently. Inside was a room that resembled a study more than a command chamber. Dark wood paneling—real wood, Arthur realized—covered the walls. A curved window looked out onto the black sweep of space. A single man stood beside the window. He turned as Arthur entered. He was older—perhaps sixty—but carried himself with the relaxed confidence of someone accustomed to giving orders that traveled light-years. “Mr. Arthur Neegan,” he said warmly. “Welcome aboard.” Arthur looked at the three soldiers. “They can stay?” he asked. The man waved a hand. “They are decorative.” The soldiers withdrew without a word. The door closed. Arthur remained standing. The man gestured to a chair. “Please.” Arthur sat cautiously. “You know my name,” Arthur said. “Care to return the favor?” The man smiled. “Director Halden Voss.” Arthur’s brow furrowed. “That name should mean something to me.” “It will.” Arthur leaned back. “So, Director Voss. Let’s skip the polite dance. You drag me off Mars because I found Azra.” “Yes.” “How much did you hear?” “All of it.” Arthur exhaled. “That’s unfortunate.” Voss clasped his hands behind his back and paced slowly. “Do you know what Azra does to radioactive decay chains, Mr. Neegan?” “I wrote the analysis.” “Yes. You did.” Arthur watched him carefully. “And yet,” Voss continued, “I suspect even you do not fully appreciate the consequences.” Arthur shrugged. “It makes drives faster and bombs nastier.” “That is the small picture.” Arthur raised an eyebrow. “The small picture?” Voss stopped pacing. “With sufficient Azra,” he said quietly, “you can force matter to decay along pathways that do not normally occur within the lifetime of the universe.” Arthur stared. “That’s theoretical.” “Yes.” “And unstable.” “Yes.” “And catastrophically dangerous.” “Yes.” Arthur leaned forward. “So why am I here?” Voss smiled again. “Because you found a deposit large enough to test the theory.” Arthur felt something cold move through his stomach. “How large do you think it is?” Voss pressed a control on the desk. A holographic projection appeared between them. Arthur recognized the scan instantly. His land plot. But the imaging had been expanded—deep, geological, penetrating kilometers beneath the surface. The Azra vein glowed like a burning river beneath the Martian crust. Arthur whispered, “That’s impossible.” “No,” said Voss. “Merely improbable.” Arthur looked up. “How many people know about this?” “Too many.” Arthur nodded slowly. “Corporations.” “Yes.” “Earth governments.” “Some.” “The Belt?” Voss smiled faintly. “Especially the Belt.” Arthur rubbed his forehead. “That explains the soldiers.” “Those were for your protection.” Arthur laughed. “That’s comforting.” Voss leaned forward. “Mr. Neegan, you are now the most politically valuable mining engineer in the solar system.” Arthur sighed. “I was afraid of that.” “Your claim sits at the center of a developing… disagreement.” “Between who?” Voss spread his hands. “Everyone.” Arthur stared at the glowing projection. “So what’s your angle?” Voss sat opposite him. “My organization would like to ensure that the Azra deposit is developed… responsibly.” Arthur gave him a long look. “Define responsibly.” Voss met his gaze. “Under our supervision.” Arthur chuckled. “Of course.” “You would retain ownership of the claim.” Arthur raised an eyebrow. “That’s generous.” “You would also receive resources no independent miner could possibly secure.” “And in return?” Voss’s voice softened. “You would allow us exclusive access to the Azra vein.” Arthur leaned back again. “And if I say no?” Voss did not hesitate. “Then someone else will ask you the same question.” Arthur nodded slowly. “With fewer manners.” “Yes.” Arthur adjusted his collar again. For the first time, he noticed his hands were shaking slightly. He looked up at Voss. “You know,” Arthur said, “two days ago my biggest problem was a drilling rig that kept jamming in basalt.” “Yes.” “And now?” Voss gestured toward the projection of the glowing Azra river beneath Mars. “Now,” he said calmly, “you are standing on the most dangerous piece of real estate in human history.” Arthur stared at the image. After a moment he said quietly, “Director Voss…” “Yes?” Arthur exhaled. “Just how many people are willing to start a war over this?” Voss smiled thinly. “Mr. Neegan,” he said, “the war has already started.” And for the first time since leaving Mars, Arthur realized that the armed men who had politely escorted him off his land might actually have been the safest company he would encounter for a very long time.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing Asimov's classic sci-fi style while delivering the requested scene structure. The author effectively establishes the retrofuturistic atmosphere through details like "synthweave" clothing, fusion drives, and diplomatic protocols that feel authentically period-appropriate. The dialogue carries the narrative effectively, revealing information naturally through conversation rather than exposition dumps. The exchanges between Arthur and the soldier, then Arthur and Voss, feel authentic and serve multiple purposes - character development, world-building, and plot advancement. The dialogue has appropriate tension and reveals character personalities efficiently. Arthur's characterization is well-handled - he's believably bewildered but not passive, showing appropriate skepticism and dry humor that makes him relatable. His progression from confusion to growing understanding of his predicament feels natural. Voss is appropriately mysterious yet professional, embodying the polite menace requested in the prompt. The world-building successfully captures Asimov's trademark approach of showing both intimate human concerns and vast political implications. The progression from Arthur's personal discomfort with formal clothing to the revelation of solar system-wide political ramifications mirrors Asimov's technique of scaling from small to large picture. The pacing is well-controlled, building tension gradually through reveals about the true scope of Arthur's discovery. The holographic projection scene effectively visualizes the stakes, and the final exchange about the war having already started provides an appropriately ominous conclusion. The prose is clean and functional without being overly ornate, matching the classic sci-fi aesthetic. Descriptive passages serve clear purposes - the ship's luxurious interior suggests power and resources, while Arthur's discomfort with his clothing humanizes him and shows his displacement from his normal environment. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the dialogue becomes slightly mechanical in service of information delivery, and a few instances where character reactions could be more deeply explored. The piece also relies somewhat heavily on familiar sci-fi tropes, though it executes them competently. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 83.8
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The darkroom always smells like vinegar and metal, like something quietly dissolving. Tonight the red safety light makes everything look unreal—Elliot’s pale hands glowing faintly over the tray, the silver rings on his fingers catching little sparks of color. I’m supposed to be the confident one here. I’m Dani, the girl who critiques professors during reviews and flirts like it’s a competitive sport. I’m the one who usually decides when something happens and how. But Elliot is standing beside me, sleeves of his oversized black sweater pushed up, carefully rocking the tray of developer like he’s trying not to wake something sleeping inside the liquid. And suddenly I don’t know how to act. “Okay,” I say, leaning over his shoulder. “Slow down a little. If you slosh it too hard the contrast gets weird.” He glances at me, dark hair falling across one eye. His style always looks like he got dressed in the back room of a haunted thrift store—black mesh under shirts, heavy boots, eyeliner smudged just enough to look accidental. The first week of class I thought he was trying very hard. Now I think maybe he just feels most like himself that way. “Like this?” he asks. He tilts the tray gently. A pale shape starts appearing on the paper—our photo from yesterday’s shoot. A self-portrait he insisted on doing: him standing under a streetlamp, coat hanging off one shoulder, expression somewhere between defiance and uncertainty. “Yeah,” I say softly. “Perfect.” We watch the image bloom. Darkrooms do this thing to conversations. The red light and the quiet make everything feel like a confession booth. Elliot clears his throat. “So,” he says. “Can I tell you something kind of… weird?” I lean back against the counter. “Elliot, we’re literally developing pictures of you looking like a Victorian ghost behind a gas station. My weird threshold is high.” He huffs out a small laugh. But he doesn’t look at me. He keeps watching the photo emerge like it might judge him. “I’ve been… thinking about gender a lot,” he says finally. My brain does a tiny, surprised somersault. “Okay,” I say carefully. He shrugs, shoulders pulling inward. “I don’t know what it means yet,” he says. “I just know that sometimes being… ‘a guy’ feels like a jacket that technically fits but I’m always tugging at the sleeves.” The tray rocks slowly in his hands. “I like some parts of it,” he continues. “But other parts feel like I’m pretending. And when I dress like this”—he gestures vaguely to the mesh shirt, the eyeliner—“people assume it’s aesthetic. But it’s not just that.” My phone buzzes once in my pocket. Probably Maya asking where I disappeared to. I ignore it. “Have you told anyone?” I ask. He shakes his head. “You’re the first.” Something tight and warm presses behind my ribs. Me. Of all people. I study him in the dim red light. His face looks younger in here, softer. Nineteen suddenly feels very nineteen. And yet he’s the one who asked to shoot a self-portrait about identity. The one who stares at contact sheets like he’s trying to decode himself. “I think it’s cool you’re thinking about it,” I say. He glances at me quickly, like he expected a different answer. “Yeah?” “Yeah.” I push off the counter and move beside him, bumping his elbow lightly. “Most people spend their whole lives wearing that jacket without noticing it’s uncomfortable.” He smiles faintly. The photo in the tray is almost fully developed now. The streetlamp halo makes his hair look like smoke. “Do you think,” he says slowly, “that it changes how people see you?” I raise an eyebrow. “Absolutely.” “No, I mean…” He hesitates. “Like… if someone liked you before.” Ah. There it is. I suddenly become hyperaware of the space between us. The warmth of his shoulder near mine. The faint scent of clove cigarettes clinging to his sweater. I have a type. Everyone knows it. Confident men. Big personalities. People who push back when I push forward. Elliot isn’t that. Elliot watches the world like he’s trying to photograph it from the inside. “I guess it depends on the person,” I say. “That’s a non-answer.” “Yeah,” I admit. He laughs softly. My phone buzzes again. Persistent. I leave it. “Can I ask you something?” I say. He nods. “Do you want me to… lead here?” I gesture between us vaguely. “Or do you want space to figure things out first?” His eyebrows lift. “That’s very diplomatic for you.” “Hey.” “You literally hijacked the lighting setup on day two of class.” “It was bad lighting,” I defend. He grins. But then he gets thoughtful again. “I don’t know yet,” he says honestly. “I just know that when I talk to you about this stuff… it doesn’t feel scary.” The red light hums softly overhead. I feel something shift inside me. Not my usual electric attraction—the kind that’s all spark and momentum. This is slower. Curious. Like watching a photograph appear in chemicals. “Good,” I say quietly. He slides the photo into the stop bath. The image stabilizes. The two of us stand there looking at it. “That’s a good portrait,” I tell him. “It’s weird seeing myself like that.” “Why?” He tilts his head. “I look… softer than I expected.” I nudge his shoulder. “You are soft.” “Is that a bad thing?” I think about every guy I’ve dated who tried very hard not to be. “No,” I say. “It’s actually kind of rare.” He looks relieved. We hang the print to dry. Our arms brush and neither of us moves away right away. I realize something then. I’m still attracted to him. But it’s different from my usual gravitational pull toward certainty. Toward people who already know exactly who they are. Elliot is in motion. And weirdly… that might be the most compelling thing about him. “You know,” I say, “for our final project we could lean into this theme.” He glances over. “What theme?” “Transformation.” He considers it. “Like… documenting the process?” “Exactly.” He smiles, small but bright. “I’d like that.” My phone buzzes a third time. Still ignored. Elliot looks at the hanging photograph again. “Thanks for listening,” he says quietly. I shrug, but I feel the moment settle somewhere important. “Hey,” I say. “Self-discovery is basically the most experimental art form there is.” He laughs. And in the red glow of the darkroom, surrounded by dripping photographs and chemical shadows, I realize neither of us knows exactly what this thing between us is going to become. But for once in my life— I’m okay not taking charge of the answer. :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The author successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive art student while showing her vulnerability when confronted with unfamiliar territory. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both as literal backdrop and metaphorical space for revelation and development. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her usual take-charge nature and her desire to give Elliot agency feels authentic and well-developed. Elliot's tentative exploration of gender identity is handled with sensitivity, avoiding both oversimplification and melodrama. His metaphor of gender as "a jacket that technically fits but I'm always tugging at the sleeves" is particularly effective. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, with realistic hesitations and the kind of careful probing that occurs in intimate conversations. The author skillfully uses subtext, particularly in the exchange about whether questioning gender identity "changes how people see you" and Dani's admission that her response was "a non-answer." The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, with effective sensory details that ground the reader in the darkroom environment. The recurring motif of photographs developing serves as an apt metaphor for both characters' evolving understanding of themselves and each other. However, the piece occasionally relies on somewhat predictable metaphors (the photograph developing paralleling self-discovery), and some moments feel slightly constructed rather than entirely organic. The ending, while satisfying, resolves perhaps a bit too neatly given the complexity of the emotions involved. The author demonstrates mature handling of attraction and identity questions without resorting to oversimplification or false resolution. The piece acknowledges uncertainty while still providing emotional satisfaction. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 18 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 18 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 77.0
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Morning light flashed on the spray of the Trevi Fountain as tourists pressed forward with phones raised. Ben had wedged himself near the edge, guidebook open, lips moving as he read. “Originally designed by Nicola Salvi in the eighteenth—” A horn blared. He looked up just in time to see a red motorcycle sliding sideways toward him. The rider swerved hard, the tires squealing across the cobblestones. The bike stopped inches from his knees. “Ma sei pazzo?!” the rider snapped, yanking off her helmet. Dark hair spilled out in a chaotic wave. Her eyes flashed. Ben froze. “I—I beg your pardon,” he said automatically. “You walk in the street like that?” she shot back in rapid Italian. “You want to die today? Bene. Very efficient. Rome traffic will help you.” “I’m terribly sorry,” he said again, clutching his guidebook. “I was reading.” “Yes, I see.” She leaned over and tapped the book with two fingers. “Very dangerous weapon.” “I’m trying to learn the city.” She studied him. Tall, rumpled, linen shirt already wrinkled. A messenger bag slung awkwardly over one shoulder. Very British confusion radiating from every pore. “Tourist?” “Academic,” he corrected weakly. “Sabbatical. History.” She groaned dramatically. “Madonna. Worse.” “I can speak some Italian,” he added. “Ah yes?” She crossed her arms. “Then say sorry again.” “Mi dispiace molto.” “Hmm.” A pause. Then a grin cracked through her irritation. “Not terrible.” A police whistle shrilled somewhere down the street. She glanced over her shoulder, then back at him. “You almost die under my bike,” she said. “So now you owe me coffee.” “I do?” “Yes.” “That seems… reversed.” She was already strapping her helmet back on. “Get on.” Ben blinked. “I beg your pardon?” “Get on the bike, Professore.” “I don’t ride motorcycles.” “Perfect day to start.” “I really should—” She revved the engine. Ben sighed. Two minutes later he was clinging awkwardly to the back of the bike as they tore through a narrow Roman street. “Is this legal?!” he shouted. “No!” --- They stopped at a tiny bar tucked between two ancient buildings. Alessandra leaned against the counter. “Due caffè,” she told the barista. Ben was still pale. “That was… very fast.” “It’s Rome. If you go slow you die.” The coffees arrived. Ben reached for his wallet. She waved him off. “My fault you almost die.” He nodded gratefully. She took the tiny cup, drank it in one swallow, then casually slipped the sugar packet holder into her jacket pocket while the barista turned away. Ben stared. “Did you just—” “Relax.” “You stole that.” She tilted her head. “Did I?” “Yes.” She grinned. “You are very observant for a man who nearly walked into traffic.” He opened his mouth, then closed it. “I… suppose it’s a small theft.” “Exactly.” She hopped off the stool. “Come. I show you Rome.” “I do have a schedule,” he said. She took his guidebook from his hands. Flipped through it. Scoffed. “Terrible.” Then she tossed it into his bag and grabbed his wrist. --- Late morning. They were running through Campo de’ Fiori market. “Try this,” Alessandra said, shoving a slice of orange into his mouth. He chewed obediently. “That’s excellent.” “Of course.” She winked at the fruit vendor and kept walking. Ben glanced back. “You didn’t pay.” “He likes me.” “Are you sure?” She held up two fingers. “Fifty percent sure.” --- Noon. They sat on the Spanish Steps eating pizza al taglio. Ben looked dazed. “I was supposed to spend today in the Vatican archives.” “Boring.” “They contain extremely important manuscripts.” “So do libraries in London.” He nodded slowly. “That’s true.” She watched him. “You like trouble, Professore.” “I don’t.” “You follow me.” “I was… swept along.” “Same thing.” A group of tourists passed. In one fluid motion Alessandra lifted a pair of cheap sunglasses from a display cart as they walked by. Ben nearly choked on his pizza. “You did it again!” She slid the sunglasses onto his face. “Better.” “I cannot wear stolen sunglasses!” “You can. I give them to you.” “That’s not how theft works.” She studied him, amused. “You worry too much.” --- Afternoon. They stood on a bridge over the Tiber. The sun was beginning to soften. Ben leaned on the railing. “This city feels… alive,” he said. “Of course.” “In England everything is orderly.” She made a face. “How sad.” “Sometimes.” She watched the water. “You always follow rules?” “Mostly.” “And today?” He hesitated. “Today I rode a motorcycle with a stranger who steals sugar containers.” She laughed. A warm, reckless sound. “You are improving.” --- A sudden crack of thunder rolled across the sky. Ben looked up. “That seems ominous.” The rain began instantly. Not drizzle. A wall of water. “Run!” Alessandra shouted. They sprinted down the street, dodging puddles and shouting pedestrians. She grabbed his arm and pulled him under the striped awning of a gelato shop. They stood there, breathless, rain hammering the pavement. Ben’s hair was soaked. Alessandra was laughing. “This city!” she said. Inside, the gelato vendor leaned out. “Due coni?” she asked Ben. “Yes, please.” Moments later they stood under the awning holding dripping cones. “Stracciatella,” she said proudly. Ben tasted his. “That’s extraordinary.” Rain splashed inches from their shoes. People ran past in chaotic waves. For a moment everything felt suspended. Alessandra leaned against the wall, studying him. “You are different than I thought.” “I nearly died this morning.” “Yes.” “I’ve committed several accessory crimes.” “Also yes.” “And yet this has been the best day I’ve had in months.” She looked slightly surprised. Then softer. “Good.” A long pause. Rain roaring around them. “You’ll return to your books tomorrow,” she said. “Probably.” “And forget this crazy Roman girl.” “I doubt that very much.” She watched him carefully. Then reached forward and adjusted the stolen sunglasses still tucked into his shirt pocket. “You keep those.” “I still feel morally conflicted.” “You look good in crime.” He laughed. The rain began to slow. She pushed off the wall. “Come.” “Where now?” She swung her helmet back on. “Last stop.” --- Sunset burned gold over the Aventine Hill. Rome stretched below them. Ben stood beside the parked motorcycle, wind tugging at his damp shirt. “It’s beautiful.” “Yes.” They stood quietly. The day finally slowing. “Alessandra,” he said, “do you often abduct tourists?” “Only the polite ones.” “And tomorrow?” She shrugged. “Maybe I work. Maybe I steal things.” “Maybe you nearly run someone else over.” “Possible.” He smiled. Then, surprising himself, he stepped closer. “May I see you again?” She studied him like a puzzle. Then she reached into his bag. Pulled out his guidebook. Slipped something inside it. “Check page 214 tonight,” she said. He frowned. “What’s on page 214?” She kicked the motorcycle to life. “You’ll see, Professore.” The engine roared. She sped down the hill and vanished into Roman traffic. Ben opened the guidebook. Page 214. Tucked inside was a small object. The silver spoon from the gelato shop. And beneath it, written in fast looping Italian: “Domani. Same chaos. Same time.”
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates two distinct characters - Alessandra, the impulsive Roman motorcycle courier with kleptomaniacal tendencies, and Ben, the polite British academic - and develops their contrasting personalities through action and dialogue rather than exposition. The structure works well, using short vignettes connected by time markers that create a cinematic quality. The pacing moves briskly from encounter to encounter, maintaining momentum while allowing character development. The dialogue feels authentic, particularly Alessandra's mix of Italian and English, and Ben's overly polite British responses that gradually loosen as the day progresses. The author handles the kleptomania subplot with appropriate subtlety - it's shown through actions (sugar packet holder, sunglasses, final spoon) rather than explicitly stated, and Ben's reactions feel genuine. The cultural clash is well-executed without falling into stereotypes, though it does rely on familiar tropes of the uptight Brit and free-spirited Italian. The prose is clean and economical, avoiding purple language while still creating vivid scenes. Rome feels present as a character itself through specific locations and atmospheric details like the sudden rainstorm. The chemistry between the characters builds naturally through shared experiences rather than forced romantic moments. However, the piece suffers from some predictability in its arc - the transformation of the buttoned-up academic through exposure to a free spirit is well-worn territory. While executed competently, it doesn't offer many surprises beyond the specific details of execution. The ending, while charming, feels somewhat inevitable rather than earned through deeper character work. The emotional complexity could be deeper - while both characters are likeable and their attraction believable, their internal lives remain somewhat surface-level. Alessandra's motivations beyond mischief-making are unclear, and Ben's academic background feels more like a plot device than integral to his character. + Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The trick to bartending on a station with lazy gravity is to pour half a finger lower than you think you should. The trick to bartending at The Last Outpost is to pretend you don’t notice anything you absolutely notice. Tonight, the gravity is drifting again. Not enough to set off alarms—just enough that every glass on my bar trembles like it’s thinking nervous thoughts. I steady a bottle with my palm. Then the door sighs open. In slumps a stranger with tentacled dreads—thin lavender tendrils that look like they’ve been through a particle storm. They pause in the doorway like the room is too bright, too loud, too everything. Then they slide onto a stool. “Vega whisky, neat,” they say quietly. “Better make that a double.” “Rough orbit?” I ask. “Something like that.” I grab the Vega. The gravity dips half a percent and the amber in the bottle wobbles like a tiny sea. “Lucky timing,” I say. “Gravity’s doing its evening stretch. Makes the first drink stronger.” “That so?” “Sure,” I say. “If the liquid climbs out of the glass and punches you in the face.” They almost smile. I pour the double and slide it over just as the station lurches a hair upward. The whisky shivers in the glass but doesn’t spill. Years of practice. “Name?” I ask. “Just passing through.” “Everybody is,” I say. “But they still have names.” They stare into the drink like it owes them money. “Call me Rill.” “Nets,” I say. “Welcome to the Outpost.” Rill knocks back half the whisky immediately. Behind them, the regulars continue their gentle chaos. Korb, a rock miner with forearms like docking pylons, is arguing with Lissa the courier about whose turn it is to feed the station’s feral maintenance drones. “I fed them last cycle!” Lissa says. “You fed one!” Korb says. “That doesn’t count as feeding a swarm.” “You try getting close to the yellow one! It bit my scanner!” “Because you named it!” “That makes it less bitey!” Gravity dips again. The foam on Korb’s beer tilts sideways. I glance back at Rill. “You look like someone who ran very fast from somewhere unpleasant,” I say. “I look like someone enjoying a drink.” “Those aren’t mutually exclusive.” They sip slower this time. “Quiet station,” they say. “You came at the calm hour.” “When’s the loud hour?” “When someone gets arrested or married.” “And which happens more?” “Depends on the cargo traffic.” Rill huffs a laugh. I start wiping the bar. The rag floats slightly during another gravity hiccup before settling again. “You work here long?” Rill asks. “Long enough to know when someone’s not just passing through.” “And what does that look like?” “Like someone who checked the exits twice before ordering.” Their tentacles twitch. “Habit.” “Sure,” I say. “Mine too.” A shadow falls across the bar. Korb. “Nets,” he says, leaning down. “You seen a skinny surveyor type come through here earlier? Pale, twitchy, owed me thirty credits.” “Describe ‘twitchy,’” I say. “Kept looking over his shoulder.” “Congratulations,” I say. “That describes half the belt.” Korb grunts. His eyes slide to Rill. “You new?” Rill doesn’t look up from their glass. “Passing through.” Korb snorts. “That’s what smugglers say.” “That’s what travelers say.” “That’s what smugglers say while traveling.” “Korb,” I say mildly, “drink your beer before gravity steals it.” Right on cue, the station dips again and his foam sloshes over the rim. He swears and stomps back to his table. Rill watches him go. “You run interference for everyone?” they ask. “Only the ones who tip.” “And the ones who don’t?” “I let Korb talk to them longer.” Another sip. “Busy place for the edge of Andromeda,” Rill says. “This isn’t the edge,” I say. “This is the pause between edges.” They consider that. Then the door opens again. Three figures step in. Not miners. Not merchants. Too clean. Boots polished. Jackets uniform-black without being actual uniforms. My stomach tightens before my brain finishes the thought. Station Authority contractors. Unofficial ones. They fan out just enough to scan the room. One of them approaches the bar. “Evening,” he says. “Depends who you ask,” I reply. He flashes a badge that’s real enough to be trouble. “We’re looking for someone.” “Everyone here is someone.” “Cephalid,” he says. “Lavender cranial tendrils. Traveling under multiple IDs.” Rill’s glass pauses halfway to their mouth. The gravity flickers again. The whisky inside ripples. The officer notices. Slowly, he turns his head. His eyes land on Rill. “Well,” he says softly. “That was convenient.” The bar goes quieter in the way rooms do when everyone pretends they’re not listening. I keep polishing the same spot. Rill sets the glass down carefully. “You’re mistaken,” they say. The officer smiles like someone who enjoys paperwork that ends badly for others. “Rill Tava,” he says. “Former navigation engineer for the Kepler Array.” “Never heard of it.” “You left three days ago,” he continues, “along with something very expensive.” “I think you’ve got the wrong—” “A quantum route seed.” Now the whole room is listening. Even Korb. Rill exhales slowly. “I didn’t steal anything.” “You disappeared with it.” “Because people started dying.” The officer’s smile tightens. “Funny,” he says. “That’s exactly why we’d like it back.” I lean on the bar. “Gentlemen,” I say, “you’re making my calm hour loud.” “This doesn’t involve you, bartender.” “Everything in my bar involves me.” The gravity stutters harder this time. Every drink trembles. A marble someone left on the counter rolls two inches and stops. Rill’s tentacles curl slightly. “You don’t want this here,” they murmur to the officer. “Oh, I think we do.” “No,” Rill says. “You really don’t.” The two other contractors shift position near the door. Blocking it. Korb mutters, “Ah hell.” I glance at Rill. “You planning to finish that drink?” I ask. They look at the whisky. “Was hoping to.” “Well,” I say, “things might get complicated.” The officer leans closer. “Stand up slowly,” he tells Rill. Rill doesn’t move. “You don’t understand,” they say quietly. “Try me.” Another gravity ripple passes through the station. This one lasts longer. Every glass on the bar begins to wobble. The whisky in Rill’s glass forms a slow, rising dome. Rill watches it. Then they look at me. “I’m really sorry about your bar,” they say. The officer frowns. “Why would—” And that’s when the station lights flicker. Hard. Somewhere deep in the Outpost, something massive powers up. :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi setting with approximately 60% dialogue in first-person present tense. The author establishes The Last Outpost as a believable waystation with effective worldbuilding details like fluctuating gravity and diverse inhabitants. The conflict emerges naturally through Rill's arrival and the subsequent appearance of Station Authority contractors seeking a stolen quantum route seed. The characterization is particularly strong. Nets emerges as a compelling narrator with a distinct voice - pragmatic, observant, and protective of her establishment. Her dialogue feels natural and reveals character through subtext. Rill is effectively portrayed as someone on the run, with their tentacled appearance and nervous behavior creating intrigue without over-explanation. The supporting characters like Korb and Lissa feel authentic to the setting. The prose style is elegant and controlled, with memorable opening lines about bartending tricks that immediately establish both setting and character voice. The author uses the fluctuating gravity as both atmospheric detail and plot device effectively. Descriptive passages are vivid without being overwrought - "tentacled dreads—thin lavender tendrils that look like they've been through a particle storm" paints a clear picture economically. The dialogue carries the story well, revealing information naturally while maintaining distinct character voices. The banter between Nets and her patrons feels lived-in and authentic. The escalating tension as the contractors arrive is well-paced, building to the cliffhanger ending without resolution, as requested. The piece maintains consistent tone throughout - cozy but with underlying tension. The worldbuilding feels organic, emerging through character interactions rather than exposition dumps. The conflict setup is intriguing, involving corporate espionage and mysterious technology without becoming overly complex for the chapter length. Minor weaknesses include some dialogue exchanges that could be tighter, and the ending, while appropriately unresolved, feels slightly abrupt. The piece could benefit from slightly more development of the stakes involved with the quantum route seed. However, these are minor issues in an otherwise well-executed piece that successfully establishes setting, character, and conflict while maintaining the requested cozy sci-fi atmosphere. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 82.2
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing I got dropped on Canal Street like a bag of trash somebody realized they didn’t want anymore. The guy in the pickup didn’t even slow all the way down when I climbed out. Just leaned across the seat, pushed the door open, and said, “This is New Orleans. Good luck, kid.” Then he peeled off before I could decide whether to say thanks or screw you. So now it’s morning—sort of. Gray, humid, loud in a way my hometown never is. Cars honking, a trumpet somewhere down the street, people yelling things that might be jokes or might be fights. I check my phone again even though I know what it says. 12%. Great. I shove it back in my pocket like maybe ignoring it will make the battery last longer. My backpack has exactly three things in it: a change of clothes, half a pack of crackers, and the $41 I took from my own birthday money jar before leaving. Two days ago I was sitting at the kitchen table telling Mom and Dad I’m trans. Now I’m two states away trying to look like I belong here. Life comes at you fast, I guess. The air smells like fried food and beer and something sour. A streetcar rattles past and everyone around me moves like they know where they’re going. I copy them, walking fast, trying not to look like a stranded alien. Internal monologue: Okay, Taylor. Step one. Don’t panic. Step two. Figure out… literally anything. A group of tourists walks by wearing matching neon shirts that say BACHELOR APOCALYPSE. One of them almost bumps into me and slurs, “Sorry, bro.” Bro. I flinch automatically, then remind myself: strangers don’t know. Strangers don’t know anything. That’s kind of the whole point of leaving. For a second the freedom of that hits me like cold water. No school. No counselor with her careful voice saying “concerning behavioral patterns.” No Dad refusing to even say the word trans like it might summon demons. But the freedom comes with a side dish of oh god where am I supposed to sleep tonight. My stomach growls. I find a bench near a little park and sit down, tearing open the crackers. They taste like dry cardboard but my body doesn’t care. “Those your last ones?” The voice comes from somewhere behind me. I turn. A kid—maybe my age, maybe a little older—is sitting on the back of the bench like a cat. Skinny. Buzzed hair dyed an uneven purple. They’re wearing a Saints hoodie even though it’s already warm. They nod at the crackers. I hesitate. Rule number one of being a runaway, according to absolutely zero experience but several movies: don’t trust anybody. But also… they look hungry. I hold out the sleeve. They hop down and take exactly two crackers. “Thanks,” they say. “Name’s Jax.” “Taylor.” They chew slowly, studying me with the kind of focus stray dogs have. “You’re new,” they say. I try to sound casual. “What makes you think that?” “You’ve checked your phone battery three times in the last minute,” Jax says. “You’re staring at everything like it’s a museum exhibit. And your shoes are still clean.” I glance down at my sneakers. They are, in fact, suspiciously clean. “Okay,” I admit. Jax grins like they just won a bet. “Welcome to the swamp.” We sit there for a minute while a guy plays saxophone across the park. The music is weirdly beautiful. “So,” Jax says eventually, “what flavor of runaway are you?” My chest tightens. “What do you mean?” “There’s categories,” they say, counting on their fingers. “Family explosion, foster care escapee, romantic tragedy, legal trouble, or ‘I thought this would be an adventure.’” I pick at the cracker crumbs in my palm. “…Family explosion.” Jax nods like that makes perfect sense. “Yeah. That’s the big one.” They don’t ask for details, which somehow makes me want to give them anyway. “I told them I’m trans,” I blurt. The word hangs there in the humid air. Jax shrugs. “Cool.” Cool. That’s it. Not a lecture. Not silence. Not Dad’s face turning to stone. Just… cool. Something tight in my chest loosens a little. “You?” I ask. Jax tilts their head. “Let’s call it ‘classified information.’” “Sounds sketchy.” “Correct.” They grin again, but there’s something behind it that looks tired. A police siren wails somewhere nearby and Jax instinctively glances toward the street. “Rule one,” they say quietly. “Don’t talk to cops unless you absolutely have to.” “I figured.” “Rule two: keep your phone alive for emergencies.” I pull mine out again like an idiot. 11%. “Yeah,” I say. “About that.” Jax whistles. “Yikes.” “I forgot my charger.” “Bold strategy.” “Look, the escape plan had a thirty‑minute planning window.” Jax considers this. “Fair.” A food truck pulls up across the street and suddenly the air smells like fried shrimp. My stomach does a dramatic whale noise. Jax hears it. “You eaten anything besides crackers?” “Nope.” They stand up and stretch. “Come on.” “Where?” “Free food if you know how to look pathetic enough.” “That’s… comforting.” We cross the street. My brain is screaming you just met this person thirty seconds ago but my legs follow anyway because hunger wins. Jax leans against the food truck window and gives the guy inside a tragic expression. “Man,” they say, “we have had a day.” The cook sighs like he’s seen this routine before. “Y’all got money?” “Emotionally, no.” He stares for a second. Then he hands out a small tray piled with fries. “Share,” he says. Jax salutes him. We sit on the curb eating fries that taste like actual heaven. For a few minutes I almost forget the disaster of the last two days. Then my phone buzzes. My heart slams into my throat. Mom. Missed calls. Five of them. A text pops up. PLEASE COME HOME. The battery drops to 10%. I stare at the screen until the words blur. “Family?” Jax asks softly. “Yeah.” “You gonna answer?” I look up at the city around us—loud, messy, full of strangers who don’t know my name yet. I think about going back. About staying. About the fact that I have absolutely no idea how dangerous this place might be. “I don’t know,” I say. Jax nods like that’s a completely normal answer. “Good,” they say. “Not knowing means you’re thinking.” They toss the last fry into their mouth and stand. “So, Taylor-from-Tennessee,” they say, slinging their backpack over one shoulder, “you wanna see where the runaways hang out?” That should probably sound terrifying. Instead it sounds like the first actual direction I’ve had all day. My phone screen dims. 9%. I shove it in my pocket and follow Jax into the noise of the city, hoping I’m not making the worst mistake of my life. :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt's requirements, successfully capturing Taylor's voice as a transgender teen runaway navigating their first day in New Orleans. The writing maintains a consistent first-person perspective with effective internal monologue that reveals Taylor's conflicted emotional state - fear, uncertainty, brief moments of hope, and the complex feelings about family contact. The characterization is nuanced and believable. Taylor's actions feel authentic for a desperate teenager - the impulsive escape, checking phone battery obsessively, sharing food despite scarcity, and following a stranger out of hunger and loneliness. Jax emerges as an intriguing secondary character with their own mysteries, and their casual acceptance of Taylor's trans identity provides a realistic moment of relief without being overly sentimental. The dialogue feels natural and age-appropriate, particularly Jax's streetwise categorization of runaways and their pragmatic survival advice. The banter has authenticity without trying too hard to sound "teen-like." The prose style effectively balances descriptive passages with internal thoughts, creating good pacing and flow. The sensory details of New Orleans are well-chosen - the humid air, mixed smells, street sounds, and visual elements like the purple-haired Jax and clean sneakers. These details ground the story in place without overwhelming the narrative. The emotional complexity is handled well, showing Taylor's simultaneous relief at anonymity and terror at isolation. The ending strikes an appropriate note of uncertainty rather than false optimism, with Taylor making a choice to continue into the unknown while acknowledging the risk. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the internal monologue feels slightly constructed rather than organic, and a few instances where the prose edges toward being overly polished for a distressed teenager's perspective. However, these don't significantly detract from the overall effectiveness. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Emotionally Complex: 16 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 16 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 16

+ Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 80.5
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing Midas woke to the sound of Chewy’s eyes turning yellow. They didn’t so much glow as flicker, like a traffic signal that had forgotten what traffic was but still felt strongly about it. Yellow meant worry, or caution, or possibly the battery pack shorting again. With Chewy it was often hard to tell. “Morning,” said Midas, sitting up in the hammock that had once been a cargo net and now functioned as a lifestyle choice. “What’s the crisis?” Chewy’s head rotated a few degrees with the quiet servo whine of ancient technology doing its level best. The koala’s felt ears were sun-faded and one was held on with three different kinds of wire. “Mission,” said Chewy. The voice came from somewhere inside his plush torso, filtered through thirty years of dust and one unfortunate pickle-jar repair. “Right,” said Midas. “That’s… less of a crisis and more of a lifestyle.” Outside their shelter—a former roadside gift shop now promoted to headquarters—wind rustled through the skeletal remains of billboard frames. One still read WELCOME TO KOALATOWN! though most of the letters had departed to pursue other opportunities. Midas rubbed his face. “Okay. Let’s deliberate.” This was an important step in any expedition. The last time they’d skipped deliberation, they had accidentally liberated three feral shopping carts and one deeply vindictive goat. Chewy’s eyes turned thoughtful blue. “Inventory,” the koala said. Midas counted on his fingers. “We have: half a jar of pickles, a rope of questionable reliability, one shovel, two boots between us—granted I’m wearing both—and the map.” The map was a crinkled brochure from the gift shop’s happier era. Someone had scribbled landmarks onto it in charcoal: the Crooked Water Tower, the Rust Forest, the Big Hole That Wasn’t There Before. Chewy’s eyes flickered green. “Objective,” he said. “Right.” Midas tapped the map. “The old grocery warehouse. If the rumors are true—” Chewy’s eyes flashed red. “—which they absolutely aren’t,” Midas continued quickly, “there might be canned food left.” Chewy considered this. His eyes cycled through several shades that could reasonably be translated as skeptical. “Plan?” he asked. Midas brightened. “Simple. We go in quietly. We look around carefully. We leave immediately if anything seems dangerous.” Chewy’s eyes turned a patient, resigned purple. They set out shortly after. The road to the warehouse passed through what used to be a parking lot and was now a thriving ecosystem of weeds, cracked asphalt, and things that had opinions about trespassers. Midas carried Chewy tucked under one arm, which was standard procedure. Chewy insisted it was tactical oversight capability. Midas suspected it was because Chewy’s legs had never actually worked, even when civilization had. “Quiet approach,” Midas whispered as they neared the building. The warehouse loomed ahead, sagging but determined. The loading bay doors hung open like someone had started saying “oh no” and never quite finished. Chewy’s eyes glowed cautious yellow. Midas stepped forward. The ground immediately collapsed. The good news was that Midas did not fall very far. The bad news was that he landed in a shopping cart. The cart rolled. “Ah,” said Midas, as gravity and poor life choices combined. They rattled down what had once been a delivery ramp and was now an excellent demonstration of momentum. The cart shot through the open warehouse door with all the dignity of a man who had completely lost control of his morning. They came to a stop by colliding gently but firmly with a stack of dusty crates. Chewy’s eyes flashed red, green, and what might have been chartreuse panic. “Well,” said Midas, disentangling himself from the cart. “Quiet enough, I think.” The inside of the warehouse smelled like old cardboard and history. Rows of shelves stretched into gloom. On one shelf sat three cans. Midas approached reverently. “Jackpot.” He brushed dust from the labels. Beans. Beans. And… something that might once have been peaches but now looked philosophically uncertain. Chewy’s eyes glowed hopeful green. “Lunch,” said Midas. Behind them, something clanked. They both turned. In the far corner of the warehouse stood a machine. It had once been a floor-cleaning robot. Now it resembled a metal turtle with abandonment issues and a spinning brush that suggested it had resolved those issues through violence. Its sensor light blinked awake. Chewy’s eyes went very red. The robot beeped. Then it charged. Midas grabbed the cans. “Strategic withdrawal!” he shouted. They ran. The robot pursued with the relentless enthusiasm of something rediscovering its purpose in life. Midas leapt over fallen shelving, ducked beneath a dangling cable, and accidentally kicked a crate of ancient glass bottles. The crash echoed like the end of civilization, which in fairness it had once been. The robot accelerated. “Door!” Midas yelled. They burst out the loading bay. The robot followed. This was where the plan improved dramatically and then immediately deteriorated. Midas saw the rope from earlier lying across the broken ramp. “Ha!” he said. He grabbed it and yanked. The rope was attached to the unstable section of asphalt they’d crossed earlier. The ground gave way. Midas, Chewy, the robot, and a surprising amount of parking lot descended together in a moderately controlled landslide. They landed at the bottom of the slope in a cloud of dust and structural regret. The robot twitched once, its brush spinning thoughtfully, then powered down with the quiet dignity of defeated machinery. Midas lay on his back staring at the sky. Chewy’s eyes flickered slowly from red to blue. “Mission outcome,” said the koala. Midas held up the cans. “All things considered? Tremendous success.” They limped home by late afternoon. Midas had acquired several new scrapes, one impressive bruise, and a deep philosophical mistrust of shopping carts. Chewy had lost more stuffing and now listed slightly to the left. They returned to the gift shop headquarters through the back door, which Midas had reinforced after the goat incident. He set the cans on their crate-table. Chewy’s eyes glowed warm green. “Dinner,” he said. Midas opened the beans with a screwdriver and a rock. They ate in companionable silence. Outside, the wind hummed through the billboard skeletons. After a while Chewy’s eyes turned curious blue. “Tomorrow mission?” he asked. Midas leaned back in the hammock. “Well,” he said. “I hear there’s a pharmacy two miles east that might still have bandages.” Chewy considered this. His eyes flickered yellow. Then red. Then, finally, green. “Plan?” he asked. Midas grinned. “Simple.” :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Terry Pratchett's distinctive style, capturing his dry wit, observational humor, and ability to find profound meaning in mundane post-apocalyptic survival. The author successfully creates a compelling dynamic between Midas and Chewy, using the animatronic koala's color-coded emotional responses as both a communication device and a source of gentle humor. The writing excels in several key areas. The voice is consistently wry and understated, with excellent comedic timing in phrases like "gravity and poor life choices combined" and "structural regret." The world-building is economical but vivid, painting a clear picture of this post-technological landscape through details like the faded "KOALATOWN!" billboard and the former gift shop headquarters. The story structure follows the prompt requirements well, beginning with deliberation, moving through a mission that goes predictably wrong, and returning the characters home "worse for wear." The mission's failure feels organic rather than forced, arising naturally from Midas's established character trait of finding trouble. Character development is subtle but effective. Midas emerges as genuinely hapless yet resilient, while Chewy's limited vocabulary and color-coded responses create surprising depth for what could have been a simple gimmick. Their relationship feels lived-in and authentic. The prose generally flows well, though there are occasional moments where the Pratchett pastiche feels slightly forced. Some descriptions border on trying too hard to capture that distinctive voice, particularly in the opening paragraphs. The dialogue, while limited by Chewy's mechanical nature, serves its purpose effectively. The story maintains coherence throughout and avoids major structural problems. The ending strikes an appropriate tone - not overly optimistic given their circumstances, but showing the characters' resilience and partnership. The circular structure, ending with plans for another mission, reinforces the ongoing nature of their survival. Minor weaknesses include some repetitive sentence structures and a few moments where the humor feels slightly manufactured rather than organic. The piece occasionally tells rather than shows, particularly in establishing character traits, though this is somewhat mitigated by the story's brevity and comedic tone. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 16 Nuanced Characters: 15 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 13 Coherent: 17 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Overwrought: 4 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 2 Unearned Transformations: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 80.2
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
:::writing The bell above the bookshop door rang hard enough to sound panicked. The man who came through it pushed the door shut behind him and leaned against it, breathing like he’d outrun a storm. For a moment he stayed there, listening. Outside, voices passed along the street, sharp and excited. “…went that way—” “…definitely him—” The man lowered his head, ran a hand through his hair, then looked up. The shop was narrow and warm. Shelves leaned with paperbacks. A cat slept in the poetry section like a folded scarf. Behind the counter, someone sat with a book open and an expression that suggested the interruption had already lost several points. “Are you hiding,” the person behind the counter said calmly, “or having a small heart attack?” “Hiding,” the man said. “Though give me a minute and we might reach phase two.” The shopkeeper closed the book, slid a finger between the pages to mark it. “I see.” Silence settled for a moment. The man pushed away from the door and brushed imaginary dust from his jacket. “Lovely shop,” he said. “You’ve been inside for twelve seconds.” “I’m a quick judge of character.” “So I see.” He offered a crooked smile. “You’re not going to ask who I am?” The shopkeeper leaned back in the chair. “You’re either a burglar who’s terribly bad at stealth,” they said, “or you’re Rhys Ashcroft.” The smile brightened instantly. “Ah,” he said. “So you do know me.” “It’s difficult not to. Your face is the size of the Millennium Centre at the moment.” Rhys sighed and ran a hand down his face. “Yes, well. Temporary insanity on the public’s part.” The shopkeeper studied him with mild interest. Up close, he looked exactly like the posters. Slightly rumpled hair. Expensive coat. The kind of smile designed to collapse national infrastructure. They were unimpressed. Outwardly. “So,” the shopkeeper said. “Should I be expecting screaming teenagers?” “Probably not. They’re usually faster.” Rhys wandered a few steps into the shop, running his fingers along the spines of books. “You’re very calm about this.” “I sell books,” the shopkeeper said. “Chaos rarely improves the browsing experience.” He glanced over his shoulder. “You really aren’t excited?” “Should I be?” “I’m internationally adored.” “You’re temporarily sheltering between ‘Gardening in Small Spaces’ and ‘The Collected Dylan Thomas.’” Rhys turned slowly, narrowing his eyes in mock suspicion. “You’re doing this on purpose.” “Doing what?” “Not being impressed.” The shopkeeper shrugged. “I haven’t seen any of your films.” Rhys blinked. “None?” “Not one.” “That’s statistically improbable.” “I read.” “So do I.” The shopkeeper gestured vaguely toward him. “Yes, but you get paid to smolder while holding swords.” Rhys laughed despite himself. “That was one film.” “Two, wasn’t it?” “…Two,” he admitted. He drifted toward the counter, picking up a paperback from a display and flipping through it without really reading. “You could at least pretend to be dazzled,” he said lightly. “It’s been a long morning.” “Would it help your recovery?” “Immensely.” The shopkeeper tilted their head, considering. “Alright,” they said flatly. “Goodness me. A celebrity.” Rhys stared. “That was terrible.” “You asked for pretending.” “Most people put a bit of effort in.” “Most people probably want something.” “And you don’t?” “I want you not to knock over the display.” Rhys glanced down. He was leaning against it. He straightened quickly. “Right. Apologies.” The shopkeeper tried not to smile. Failed slightly. Rhys noticed. “Ah,” he said softly. “There it is.” “What?” “A crack in the armor.” “There’s no armor.” “You smiled.” “That was pity.” “I’ll take it.” He set the book down. Up close, he smelled faintly of rain and something expensive and warm. The shopkeeper suddenly found the counter very interesting. “You’re hiding from photographers,” they said briskly. “How long does that usually take?” “Until they get bored.” “And how long does that usually take?” “…Not encouragingly short.” They sighed. “Well. As long as you don’t start signing things without buying them.” Rhys leaned his elbows on the counter. “You’re Welsh.” “Yes.” “Born here?” “In Cardiff, yes.” He looked around the shop again, softer this time. “Nice life.” The shopkeeper snorted. “You say that like it’s quaint.” “It is quaint.” “It’s rent and inventory spreadsheets.” Rhys smiled. “I meant the quiet.” The shopkeeper hesitated. There was something unexpectedly sincere in the way he said it. Not the polished interview voice. “You don’t get much of that?” “Not since about 2016.” “Tragic.” “You’re enjoying this.” “A little.” Rhys tilted his head, studying them. “You know,” he said slowly, “most people try quite hard to make me like them.” “That sounds exhausting.” “It’s lovely, actually.” “I’m sure it is.” “But you’re not.” “No.” “Why not?” The shopkeeper shrugged. “You already have millions of people liking you. It seems greedy to add one more.” Rhys laughed again, quieter this time. “You’re dangerous.” “I sell secondhand paperbacks.” “You wound egos with surgical precision.” “Yours seems sturdy.” Rhys hesitated. Then, unexpectedly honest: “Not particularly.” The shopkeeper looked up. For a moment neither of them spoke. Outside, a car door slammed. Voices faded farther down the street. Rhys exhaled slowly. “Anyway,” he said lightly, recovering himself. “If you’re not a fan, I suppose my usual charm offensive is wasted.” “Probably.” “That’s inconvenient.” “Why?” “Because it’s my primary skill.” The shopkeeper felt warmth creeping up their neck and hated it. “Then you’re in trouble.” Rhys leaned a little closer across the counter. “Or,” he said, softer, “I might have to try being interesting instead.” Their eyes met. The shopkeeper immediately looked away and reached for the book they’d been reading earlier. “You could start,” they said, flipping it open, “by buying something.” Rhys grinned slowly. “Ah,” he said. “Progress.” “I’m running a business.” “And here I thought you were immune to my charms.” “I am.” Rhys picked up the same paperback again. “What’s this one about?” “I have no idea.” “You sell books you haven’t read?” “Thousands of them.” “Scandalous.” “Are you buying it or flirting with it?” Rhys set it on the counter. “I’ll take it.” The shopkeeper rang it up. “Cash or card?” Rhys slid his card across. Their fingers brushed. Both of them paused for a fraction too long. The shopkeeper cleared their throat. “Receipt?” “Yes, please.” They handed it over. Rhys took it, lingering a second. “You know,” he said casually, “if I needed to hide from paparazzi again…” “Yes?” “This seems like a good place.” The shopkeeper met his eyes. “Well,” they said coolly, “next time try knocking less dramatically.” Rhys smiled. “I’ll practice.” The bell over the door jingled as he stepped back outside. The shop grew quiet again. Behind the counter, the shopkeeper stared at the door for several seconds. Then they pressed the book they’d been pretending to read flat on the counter and muttered to themselves: “Oh, that is absolutely not happening.” :::
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and adherence to the prompt requirements. The author successfully creates a meet-cute scenario in a Welsh bookstore with two well-defined characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue. The actor Rhys is portrayed as charming yet insecure, desperately needing validation, while the bookstore owner maintains cool detachment that masks growing attraction. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with distinct voices for each character. Rhys speaks with practiced charm and occasional vulnerability, while the shopkeeper delivers dry, understated responses that reveal wit and intelligence. The banter feels natural and serves the dual purpose of character development and romantic tension building. The setting is efficiently established without excessive description, and the Welsh location feels authentic rather than forced. The author shows restraint in world-building, focusing on character interaction rather than elaborate scene-setting. The romantic tension builds organically through their verbal sparring, with the shopkeeper's immunity to Rhys's usual charm creating genuine intrigue for both characters. The moment of finger-brushing during the transaction provides appropriate physical tension without being heavy-handed. However, the piece suffers from some predictable beats. The setup of a celebrity hiding from paparazzi is familiar territory, and while executed competently, it doesn't offer surprising twists on the trope. The shopkeeper's final internal monologue, while character-appropriate, feels somewhat expected given the romantic genre conventions. The prose is clean and functional but occasionally lacks distinctive flair. Some exchanges, while serviceable, don't sparkle with the wit that would elevate the piece from competent to memorable. The emotional complexity is present but could be deeper - both characters remain somewhat within expected parameters for their archetypes. The pacing works well, building from initial tension through growing attraction to a satisfying conclusion that promises future encounters without forcing premature resolution. The author demonstrates good instincts for romantic timing and tension management.
